shiftMode: Call for help early; assign airway, meds, monitor, suction, backup airway, and post-intubation sedation roles.
Preoxygenate aggressively; consider NC apneic oxygenation, flush-rate NRB, NIV, or BVM with PEEP depending physiology.
Have suction in hand or immediately reachable before first attempt.
Choose first-pass plan and backup plan before pushing meds: bougie, video/direct, supraglottic, cricothyrotomy.
Physiology kills before anatomy: optimize hypotension, hypoxemia, severe acidosis, RV failure, and hyperkalemia when possible.
Confirm with continuous waveform capnography; auscultation alone is not enough.
Do not forget post-intubation analgesia/sedation and ventilator reassessment.
indications: Failure to oxygenate or ventilate
Inability to protect airway
Expected clinical deterioration requiring airway control
Severe agitation or injury requiring controlled oxygenation/ventilation
Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest airway management when appropriate
contraindications: No absolute contraindication in a true airway emergency
Anticipated impossible oral intubation should trigger alternate plan early
Severe physiologic instability may require resuscitation before induction when time allows
High cervical spine concern requires manual in-line stabilization rather than paralysis avoidance alone
anatomy: Align oral, pharyngeal, and laryngeal axes when using direct laryngoscopy; ear-to-sternal-notch positioning helps many adults.
Identify epiglottis, arytenoids, posterior cartilages, and cords. Do not advance through unidentified tissue.
In obesity or pregnancy, ramp aggressively rather than placing the patient flat.
equipment: Monitor: cardiac monitor, pulse ox, BP cycling, waveform capnography ready
Preoxygenation setup: NRB, nasal cannula, BVM with PEEP, NIV as needed
Video laryngoscope and/or direct laryngoscope with working light
ET tubes: planned size plus one size smaller
Cricothyrotomy kit available for high-risk airway
Induction and paralytic medications drawn up and labeled
positioning: Place patient ear-to-sternal-notch when possible.
Ramped position for obesity, pregnancy, or poor neck mobility.
Head elevated when tolerated to prolong safe apnea time.
Manual in-line stabilization if cervical spine injury is a concern.
steps: Assess airway difficulty and physiologic risk.
Preoxygenate and optimize hemodynamics before induction when possible.
Verbalize primary and backup airway plan.
Prepare equipment, suction, capnography, tube securement, and post-intubation medications.
Administer induction and paralytic medications when ready.
Perform laryngoscopy with deliberate epiglottis and glottic identification.
Pass tube under direct or video visualization; use bougie early if view is limited.
Inflate cuff and connect BVM or ventilator.
Confirm continuous waveform capnography and bilateral chest rise; check for right mainstem.
Secure tube, document depth, obtain chest x-ray when appropriate, and reassess ventilator/sedation.
ultrasound: Ultrasound can be used as an adjunct for tube confirmation in some settings, but continuous waveform capnography remains the practical standard when available.
confirmation: Continuous waveform capnography with persistent waveform
Bilateral chest rise and breath sounds
Absent gastric insufflation sounds
Appropriate oxygenation/ventilation response
Chest x-ray for depth and complications when clinically appropriate
troubleshooting: Desaturation: stop, oxygenate with BVM/PEEP, improve mask seal, use oral/nasal airway, recruit help.
Poor view: optimize position, suction, external laryngeal manipulation, change blade, use bougie early.
Cannot intubate/cannot oxygenate: supraglottic airway or surgical airway pathway immediately.
Post-intubation hypotension: check tube depth, breath stacking/auto-PEEP, tension pneumothorax, sedation effect, occult shock; start fluids/pressors as appropriate.
High peak pressures: consider bronchospasm, tube obstruction, mainstem, pneumothorax, or ventilator mismatch.
complications: Failed airway requiring supraglottic or surgical airway
aftercare: Start analgesia/sedation immediately unless intentionally held for neurologic assessment.
Set ventilator based on disease state; reassess plateau pressure, oxygenation, ventilation, and hemodynamics.
Obtain chest x-ray when appropriate to confirm depth and evaluate complications.
Reassess tube depth after transport or major position changes.
documentation: Endotracheal intubation performed for airway protection/respiratory failure/anticipated clinical deterioration.
Consent obtained when feasible; emergent procedure performed when delay would risk patient harm.
Patient preoxygenated and monitored with cardiac monitor, pulse oximetry, blood pressure, and capnography.
Medications administered as documented in MAR.
Endotracheal tube placed under visualization and secured at documented depth.
Placement confirmed with continuous waveform capnography and clinical assessment.
Patient tolerated procedure; complications, if any, documented separately.
seniorPearls: Your first attempt should be your best attempt. Do not burn an attempt while still setting up.
If the patient is crashing from physiology, a beautiful view of the cords will not save them by itself.
Have the post-intubation plan ready before induction; the tube is not the finish line.
references: Walls RM, Murphy MF. Manual of Emergency Airway Management.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
ACEP and critical care airway education resources; verify local airway policy and medication protocols.

visualAssets.title: Airway alignment axes
visualAssets.subtitle: Oral, pharyngeal, and tracheal axes aligned via sniffing/ramped position. Ear-to-sternal-notch alignment optimizes the view.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Obese patients require ramping, not just a pillow under the head.

visualAssets.title: Laryngoscopic view grades
visualAssets.subtitle: Cormack-Lehane grades I–IV. Grade I: full cords visible. Grade IV: no glottic structures seen. Bougie or video laryngoscopy for grade III–IV.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Grade III–IV views require immediate backup plan. Do not persist with direct laryngoscopy.

shiftMode: Confirm indication, site, side, anticoagulation/coagulopathy context, and whether this should be triple lumen, introducer, or dialysis catheter instead.
Use full sterile barrier: cap, mask, sterile gown/gloves, wide chlorhexidine prep, large drape.
Ultrasound before puncture: identify vein, artery, thrombus, depth, compressibility, and safest trajectory.
Needle tip visualization matters. Do not just watch tissue move.
Never dilate unless the wire passes smoothly and you are confident it is venous.
Keep control of the wire at all times.
Confirm placement and evaluate for complications per site and institutional practice.
indications: Vasopressor or irritant medication administration
Poor peripheral access with need for reliable central access
Central venous pressure monitoring when clinically useful
Massive transfusion or high-flow resuscitation when appropriate catheter chosen
Temporary access while definitive access is arranged
contraindications: Patient refusal when non-emergent
Infection or burn over intended insertion site
Thrombosis or distorted anatomy at intended site
Uncorrected coagulopathy is a relative risk; balance against urgency and site choice
Do not place a standard triple lumen when the clinical need is dialysis-level flow or rapid large-volume infusion
anatomy: IJ: vein is commonly lateral/anterior to carotid but anatomy varies; verify with ultrasound.
Femoral: NAVEL lateral-to-medial reminder is imperfect; ultrasound beats memory.
Subclavian/axillary access has pneumothorax and noncompressible bleeding considerations.
Always distinguish compressible vein from pulsatile/noncompressible artery before puncture.
equipment: Central line kit with catheter, wire, needle, scalpel, dilator, and caps
Sterile gown, gloves, mask, cap, and large sterile drape
Sterile probe cover and sterile gel
Local anesthetic, syringe, and small needle
Biopatch or antimicrobial dressing per local policy
Chest x-ray or confirmation workflow if IJ/subclavian/axillary
positioning: IJ: Trendelenburg if tolerated, head slightly turned away, avoid excessive rotation.
Femoral: supine, leg slightly abducted and externally rotated if tolerated.
Optimize monitor visibility and ultrasound position before sterile prep.
Place the ultrasound screen in your line of sight, not behind your shoulder.
steps: Confirm indication and choose site based on urgency, anatomy, infection risk, bleeding risk, and clinical need.
Perform ultrasound survey before opening the kit.
Prep and drape using full sterile barrier technique.
Anesthetize skin and deeper tissue along planned tract.
Cannulate vein under ultrasound guidance, maintaining needle tip awareness.
Confirm venous blood return and pass guidewire smoothly without force.
Use ultrasound or other method to confirm wire in vessel when possible.
Make small skin nick while controlling wire.
Dilate over wire without force; stop if resistance or uncertainty.
Advance catheter over wire, remove wire while maintaining catheter control, and confirm wire is out intact.
Aspirate and flush all ports.
Secure catheter and apply sterile dressing.
Confirm position and assess for complications per site and local policy.
ultrasound: Use short-axis for vessel identification; consider long-axis for needle tip tracking if skilled.
Compress vein before sterile prep to evaluate patency and thrombus.
Confirm guidewire in the vein before dilation when feasible.
For IJ, check lung sliding afterward as a rapid pneumothorax screen when skilled, but follow local confirmation standards.
confirmation: All ports aspirate and flush easily
Guidewire removed intact
Catheter secured with sterile dressing
Appropriate imaging/confirmation obtained based on site
No immediate evidence of arterial placement, pneumothorax, hematoma, or malposition
troubleshooting: Wire will not pass: stop, do not force; check needle position and angle, aspirate, use ultrasound, or repuncture.
Bright pulsatile blood or arterial waveform: stop and manage as arterial puncture/cannulation based on catheter size and site.
Resistance with dilator: stop; confirm wire path and vessel location before proceeding.
No blood return from one port: check clamp/cap, flush gently, reposition; do not force against resistance.
Unstable after line: consider pneumothorax, bleeding, arrhythmia, air embolism, or unrelated shock progression.
complications: Pneumothorax or hemothorax, especially with IJ/subclavian/axillary approaches
aftercare: Label line and date dressing per local policy.
Review imaging/confirmation before using for high-risk infusions if required by site/policy.
Remove unnecessary lines as soon as clinically appropriate.
Reassess insertion site for bleeding or hematoma after placement.
documentation: Central venous catheter placed for documented clinical indication.
Consent obtained when feasible; emergent need documented if consent not possible.
Site and side confirmed; sterile technique with full barrier precautions used.
Ultrasound guidance used to identify vessel and guide cannulation.
Guidewire passed without resistance and removed intact.
All ports aspirated and flushed; catheter secured with sterile dressing.
Placement confirmation and immediate complication assessment documented.
seniorPearls: Most catastrophic central line errors happen after venous puncture, not before it: wire control, dilation, and confirmation are where discipline matters.
If the wire does not pass smoothly, the answer is almost never more force.
Open the exact catheter you need. A triple lumen is not a Cordis and not a dialysis catheter.
references: Miller's Anesthesia and major critical care procedural texts for central venous access principles.
CDC guidance for prevention of intravascular catheter-related infections.
Society and institutional ultrasound-guided vascular access policies; verify local central line bundle requirements.

visualAssets.title: IJ ultrasound probe orientation
visualAssets.subtitle: Short-axis view of IJ vein and carotid artery. Probe marker toward patient's right. Vein is compressible, lateral and superficial to the artery.

visualAssets.title: Sternocleidomastoid triangle
visualAssets.subtitle: The IJ vein runs between the two heads of the SCM. The apex of the triangle at the clavicle is the traditional landmark insertion point.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Always use ultrasound guidance. Landmark-only technique increases complication risk.

visualAssets.title: Wire position confirmation
visualAssets.subtitle: After wire insertion, confirm intravascular position with ultrasound visualization in the IJ and cardiac view for right atrial wire tip.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Never dilate or place the catheter without confirmed wire position.

shiftMode: Document pre-block neurovascular status before anesthetizing the digit.
Use small-volume local anesthetic and inject slowly.
Avoid injecting directly through infected tissue when possible.
Aspirate before injection.
Give the block time to work before starting repair.
Reassess anesthesia and distal perfusion before procedure.
indications: Laceration repair
Nailbed injury
Foreign body removal
Paronychia or minor drainage procedure when appropriate
Fracture or dislocation analgesia adjunct
contraindications: Patient refusal
Allergy to planned anesthetic
Infection over injection path when alternate approach is available
Major vascular compromise requiring urgent surgical evaluation should not be delayed
anatomy: Digital nerves run along the medial and lateral aspects of the digit with accompanying vessels.
Injection at the base of the digit can anesthetize both dorsal and volar branches depending technique.
Avoid circumferential high-volume injection that creates unnecessary tissue pressure.
equipment: Procedure tray for definitive repair or drainage
positioning: Position hand or foot comfortably on a stable surface.
Expose the entire digit and remove rings before swelling worsens.
Keep the patient from watching if that increases anxiety.
steps: Confirm digit, injury, and pre-procedure neurovascular status.
Clean injection site.
Insert needle at base of digit along dorsal-lateral aspect depending chosen technique.
Aspirate before injection.
Inject slowly while withdrawing or redirecting based on technique.
Repeat on opposite side if needed.
Wait several minutes and test anesthesia before starting procedure.
Proceed with repair, drainage, reduction, or nail procedure once anesthesia is adequate.
confirmation: Patient reports improved pain or numbness in target distribution
Pinch or light touch testing confirms anesthesia before procedure
Distal perfusion remains intact
troubleshooting: Incomplete anesthesia: wait longer, identify missed distribution, supplement carefully with small volume.
Severe pain with injection: slow down, reposition, buffer/warm anesthetic if available.
Poor perfusion after injection: remove constrictive dressing/rings, reassess volume/epinephrine use, escalate if persistent.
complications: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity, rare with typical digital block volumes
aftercare: Warn patient that numbness will persist after discharge depending anesthetic used.
Protect anesthetized digit from heat, trauma, and biting injury.
Provide wound-specific aftercare and return precautions.
documentation: Digital nerve block performed for analgesia/anesthesia of the affected digit.
Verbal consent obtained and site confirmed.
Pre-procedure neurovascular status documented.
Skin prepped and local anesthetic injected using clean/sterile technique after aspiration.
Adequate anesthesia achieved prior to procedure.
Patient tolerated block without immediate complication.
seniorPearls: Slow injection is the difference between a tolerable block and a patient who never trusts you again.
Do the neurovascular exam before the block. Afterward, you created numbness on purpose.
Remove rings early. Waiting until the finger looks like a sausage is amateur hour.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify local anesthetic maximum dosing and institutional policy.

visualAssets.title: Digital block injection sites
visualAssets.subtitle: Visual slot for dorsal/lateral injection sites, volume caution, and neurovascular anatomy.

shiftMode: This is an oxygenation rescue, not a perfect cosmetic airway.
Call failed airway out loud and assign someone to continue oxygenation attempts while you prep the neck.
Landmark before the airway is lost if the airway looks high risk.
Prep wide if time allows; do not let sterile perfection delay oxygenation in a true cannot-oxygenate crisis.
Use a simple scalpel-finger-bougie/tube workflow unless your institution has a specific kit/process.
Confirm with ETCO2 and chest rise; secure aggressively.
indications: Cannot intubate/cannot oxygenate scenario
Failed airway with rapidly worsening hypoxemia
Upper airway obstruction where oral/nasal approach is not feasible
Massive facial trauma or airway contamination when standard approaches fail
contraindications: Young children require special consideration; needle/surgical approach varies by age and institutional policy
Laryngeal fracture or distorted neck anatomy may make landmarks unreliable
Coagulopathy is a caution but not a reason to allow death from hypoxemia
anatomy: Cricothyroid membrane lies between thyroid cartilage and cricoid cartilage.
Stabilize the larynx with the non-dominant hand.
In obesity or edema, make a generous vertical skin incision first to find the membrane.
equipment: Cuffed tube, often 6.0 adult ETT or trach/cric tube per kit
Hook or tracheal dilator if available
Backup commercial cric kit if available
positioning: Supine when possible
Neck extended unless contraindicated; neutral if cervical spine concern
Stand at head or side based on training and access
Expose neck widely
steps: Identify and stabilize larynx.
Make vertical skin incision if landmarks are difficult or time allows.
Make horizontal incision through cricothyroid membrane.
Open tract with finger, scalpel handle, or dilator depending technique.
Pass bougie toward feet/trachea.
Railroad cuffed tube over bougie.
Inflate cuff, ventilate, confirm ETCO2 and chest rise.
Secure tube and reassess oxygenation/ventilation.
confirmation: Continuous waveform ETCO2
Bilateral chest rise
Improving oxygen saturation if circulation intact
Ability to ventilate without massive leak
Chest x-ray after stabilization
troubleshooting: Cannot find membrane: vertical incision, bluntly dissect with finger, re-landmark.
Tube will not pass: rotate, use smaller tube, confirm bougie is intratracheal.
No ETCO2: reassess placement, obstruction, circuit, and circulation.
Bleeding: suction aggressively; oxygenation remains priority.
aftercare: Secure extremely well; accidental dislodgement can be catastrophic.
Arrange definitive airway/ENT/trauma/ICU follow-up.
Obtain imaging after stabilization.
Document failed airway context and rescue rationale.
documentation: Emergent cricothyrotomy performed for failed airway/cannot intubate-cannot oxygenate scenario.
Procedure performed due to immediate threat to life when delay would risk patient harm.
Neck exposed/prepped as clinical situation allowed.
Cricothyroid membrane identified; surgical airway established using documented technique.
Placement confirmed with waveform capnography and clinical assessment.
Complications and post-procedure plan documented.
seniorPearls: The decision is usually harder than the cut; say it out loud early.
A small timid incision is a common failure mode. Make enough room to work.
Once oxygenation is failing, this is not the moment for committee discussion.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify local institutional policy, kit contents, consent requirements, and credentialing expectations.

visualAssets.title: Cricothyroid membrane palpation
visualAssets.subtitle: Palpate the thyroid cartilage notch, slide inferiorly to the soft depression before the cricoid ring. This is the cricothyroid membrane — the incision target.
visualAssets.caption: Illustration pending clinical review. Use the visual asset workflow before bundling final artwork.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Vertical skin incision reduces risk of vessel injury. Horizontal membrane incision.

visualAssets.title: Anterior neck danger zones
visualAssets.subtitle: Anterior jugular veins run laterally. Thyroid isthmus sits inferior to the cricothyroid membrane. Superior and inferior laryngeal arteries course near the membrane edges.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Stay midline. Lateral deviation risks vascular injury.

shiftMode: Confirm side and indication before incision.
Have suction/water seal system set up before the tube goes in.
Use safe triangle when possible; go over the rib, not under.
Finger sweep before tube advancement; do not blindly shove a tube through soft tissue.
Direct tube apically for pneumothorax and posterior/basilar for fluid when feasible.
Secure with strong suture and occlusive dressing; reassess breath sounds and imaging.
indications: Pneumothorax requiring drainage
Hemothorax
Empyema or complicated effusion when appropriate
Traumatic arrest/trauma decompression pathway per local protocol
contraindications: Uncorrected coagulopathy is a caution when non-emergent
Adhesions/prior surgery may increase risk
Diaphragm/liver/spleen injury risk with low placement
Small stable pneumothorax may not require tube
anatomy: Classic safe triangle: lateral border pectoralis major, anterior border latissimus dorsi, line above nipple/5th intercostal space, apex axilla.
Neurovascular bundle runs under rib; enter over the superior aspect of the rib.
Avoid low lateral placement that can enter abdomen.
equipment: Appropriate tube size or pigtail if selected
Ultrasound if fluid or anatomy uncertain
positioning: Arm abducted or hand behind head if tolerated
Patient supine or slight lateral depending indication
Expose entire hemithorax and confirm side
steps: Confirm side, indication, consent/emergency rationale.
Mark site and prep/drape widely.
Anesthetize skin, periosteum, and pleura generously.
Incise parallel to rib.
Bluntly dissect with Kelly over rib into pleural space.
Perform finger sweep to confirm intrapleural entry and adhesions.
Advance tube with clamp/finger guidance.
Connect to drainage system and suction/water seal as indicated.
Suture, dress, obtain imaging, and reassess patient.
ultrasound: Use ultrasound for pleural fluid localization and to avoid solid organs when placing for effusion.
For pneumothorax, ultrasound can support diagnosis with absent lung sliding, but clinical context matters.
confirmation: Air/fluid return when appropriate
Improved respiratory/hemodynamic status
Tidaling/air leak assessment
Chest x-ray or CT confirmation
Document tube depth and output
troubleshooting: No rush of air/fluid: confirm pleural entry, tube holes in chest, and system connection.
Persistent pneumothorax: check for dislodgement, kink, insufficient depth, bronchopleural fistula, or need second tube.
Bleeding: quantify output, notify surgery/trauma if high-volume or ongoing.
Pain: reassess analgesia; chest tubes hurt.
aftercare: Post-procedure imaging.
Trend output and air leak.
Analgesia plan.
Suction/water seal management per indication and local policy.
Escalate early for massive hemothorax or persistent instability.
documentation: Tube thoracostomy performed for documented indication and side.
Consent obtained when feasible; emergent rationale documented if applicable.
Site prepped and draped; local anesthesia used.
Blunt dissection performed over rib with finger confirmation of pleural entry.
Tube placed and connected to drainage system; secured and dressed.
Placement confirmed clinically and with imaging as appropriate; immediate complications documented.
seniorPearls: The most preventable disaster is being too low.
Finger sweep is the safety check; skipping it is how tubes end up in soft tissue.
Have the drainage system ready before pleura is entered.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.

visualAssets.title: Safe triangle of insertion
visualAssets.subtitle: Bordered by the anterior border of latissimus dorsi, lateral border of pectoralis major, a line superior to the horizontal level of the nipple, and the apex of the axilla. 4th–5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line.
visualAssets.caption: Illustration pending clinical review. Use the visual asset workflow before bundling final artwork.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Insert above the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle running along the inferior rib margin.

visualAssets.title: Over-the-rib insertion
visualAssets.subtitle: The intercostal neurovascular bundle (vein, artery, nerve) runs along the inferior margin of each rib. Always pass the tube over the superior border of the lower rib.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Inferior rib entry risks intercostal artery laceration and hemorrhage.

shiftMode: Best for selected pneumothorax/effusion scenarios, not every bloody trauma chest.
Use ultrasound for fluid; confirm anatomy before puncture.
Seldinger discipline matters: never lose wire control and do not force dilation.
Confirm all side holes are intrathoracic before connecting drainage.
Secure well; small catheters dislodge easily.
If the patient is unstable or draining blood, escalate early rather than treating the pigtail as definitive.
indications: Selected pneumothorax
Pleural effusion requiring drainage
Empyema/complicated fluid when appropriate and within local practice
contraindications: Massive hemothorax or unstable trauma may need large-bore tube/surgical approach
Unclear anatomy or unsafe window
Coagulopathy is a caution for non-emergent drainage
Loculated fluid may require image guidance or specialty input
anatomy: Enter over rib to avoid neurovascular bundle.
For fluid, choose ultrasound-confirmed pocket away from diaphragm and solid organs.
For pneumothorax, anterior/superior location may be selected depending patient position and local practice.
equipment: Ultrasound with sterile cover/gel if fluid or anatomy uncertain
positioning: Position based on target: upright/lateral for fluid, supine/semi-upright for pneumothorax
Expose and prep widely
Keep patient still during wire/dilator steps

Prep/drape and anesthetize tract to pleura.
Enter pleural space with needle; confirm air/fluid return.
Advance guidewire while maintaining control.
Nick skin and dilate gently over wire.
Advance catheter over wire; remove wire while maintaining catheter position.
Confirm pigtail formed and side holes intrathoracic.
Connect to drainage system and secure/dress.
Obtain imaging and reassess.
ultrasound: Ultrasound is strongly preferred for pleural fluid localization.
Identify diaphragm, liver/spleen, lung, and depth to pleural space before puncture.
confirmation: Air/fluid drainage
Improved symptoms/oxygenation if clinically relevant
Chest imaging for position and residual pneumothorax/fluid
No expanding subcutaneous emphysema or bleeding

Poor drainage: check kink, stopcock, clot/debris, catheter depth, and whether side holes are outside chest.

aftercare: Secure and label drain.
Track output and air leak.
Flush only per local protocol.
Repeat imaging based on indication and clinical course.
documentation: Pigtail pleural catheter placed for documented indication and side.
Consent/emergent rationale documented.
Site selected with imaging/ultrasound as appropriate.
Seldinger technique used with guidewire control maintained.
Catheter connected to drainage system, secured, and dressed.
Placement and complications assessed clinically and by imaging as appropriate.
seniorPearls: Pigtails are less dramatic than chest tubes but not less capable of causing harm.

Small catheter plus bad securement equals accidental removal.

visualAssets.title: Pleural ultrasound targeting
visualAssets.subtitle: Curvilinear probe at the posterior axillary line. Identify the diaphragm, lung, and effusion/pneumothorax. Mark the insertion site at the point of maximal fluid above the diaphragm.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Always confirm diaphragm position. Do not insert below the diaphragm.

visualAssets.title: Seldinger technique setup
visualAssets.subtitle: Needle → wire → dilator → pigtail catheter over wire. Confirm wire position with ultrasound before dilation. The pigtail curl should deploy in the pleural space.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Never advance the dilator without confirmed wire position.

shiftMode: This is a temporizing maneuver for suspected tension physiology.
Do not wait for imaging in a crashing patient with compatible findings.
Use appropriate site and catheter length; short catheters fail.
Preferred sites vary; know your local protocol: 4th/5th anterior or mid-axillary, or 2nd ICS midclavicular.
Definitive tube thoracostomy usually follows if tension pneumothorax is likely.
If one decompression attempt fails and tension remains plausible, execute the backup plan immediately: finger/tube thoracostomy or alternate decompression site per protocol.
indications: Suspected tension pneumothorax with hemodynamic or respiratory compromise
Traumatic arrest with decompression pathway
Peri-intubation or ventilated patient crashing with unilateral concern
contraindications: No true contraindication in life-threatening suspected tension physiology
Stable patient with uncertain diagnosis should get better diagnostic confirmation when possible
anatomy: Enter over the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.
Lateral sites need attention to breast tissue, body habitus, and tube length.
2nd ICS MCL can fail in thick chest walls and risks malposition if too medial.
equipment: Long large-bore angiocath or decompression device per local protocol
positioning: Expose chest rapidly
Arm up if using lateral site and feasible
Do not delay for perfect positioning if peri-arrest
steps: Identify correct side based on clinical scenario.
Choose site per local protocol and anatomy.
Prep quickly if time allows.
Insert catheter over rib perpendicular to chest wall until pleural entry/air release.
Advance catheter, remove needle, secure.
Immediately reassess and prepare definitive thoracostomy if indicated.
ultrasound: POCUS can rapidly support diagnosis if it does not delay decompression in a crashing patient.
confirmation: Immediate clinical improvement may occur but is not required for diagnosis to have been reasonable.
Reassess BP, oxygenation, breath sounds, ventilator pressures, and chest rise.
Follow with chest tube when indicated.
troubleshooting: No improvement: wrong side/site, catheter too short, kinked catheter, hemothorax, tamponade, tube problem, or non-thoracic shock.
Catheter occludes: proceed to definitive thoracostomy.
Persistent instability: reassess whole resuscitation, not just the chest.
Rescue: if initial decompression fails and clinical picture still supports tension, proceed directly to finger or tube thoracostomy rather than repeating needle attempts.
complications: Misplacement into soft tissue or abdomen
aftercare: Definitive chest tube if tension pneumothorax remains likely.
Imaging once stabilized.
Document rationale for emergent decompression.
documentation: Needle decompression performed emergently for suspected tension pneumothorax with documented clinical findings.
Site and side documented.
Catheter placed using anatomic landmarks/local protocol.
Patient response reassessed immediately.
Definitive thoracostomy/imaging plan documented.
seniorPearls: A needle is a bridge, not the destination.
If the patient is dying and the story fits, decompression is a clinical decision.
Failure is common enough that you must reassess immediately.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.

visualAssets.title: Decompression landmarks
visualAssets.subtitle: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (anterior) or 4th–5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line (lateral). Lateral approach preferred in muscular or obese patients.
visualAssets.caption: Illustration pending clinical review. Use the visual asset workflow before bundling final artwork.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Standard 5 cm catheter may be insufficient in obese patients. Consider longer catheter or finger thoracostomy.

visualAssets.title: Medial danger zone
visualAssets.subtitle: Internal mammary artery runs 1–2 cm lateral to the sternum. Stay in the midclavicular line to avoid it. The intercostal bundle runs along the inferior rib margin.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Too medial risks internal mammary artery. Too low risks abdominal contents.

shiftMode: This is for tamponade physiology, not just an effusion on ultrasound.
Use ultrasound to identify the safest fluid pocket and needle path whenever possible.
Have resuscitation, pressors, blood, and surgical backup moving in parallel when indicated.
Advance under visualization if possible; aspirate continuously or frequently.
Even small drainage volumes can dramatically improve tamponade physiology.
Keep the whole obstructive shock differential active; tamponade can coexist with bleeding, tension pneumothorax, or profound hypovolemia.
indications: Cardiac tamponade with hemodynamic compromise
Peri-arrest/arrest with ultrasound evidence of tamponade in appropriate context
Diagnostic/therapeutic drainage when benefits outweigh risks
contraindications: No absolute contraindication in life-threatening tamponade
Aortic dissection/contained rupture requires extreme caution and specialist involvement when feasible
Coagulopathy is a caution but may be outweighed by shock
anatomy: Potential approaches include subxiphoid, parasternal, or apical based on best ultrasound window.
Avoid internal mammary vessels with parasternal approach.
Needle trajectory should target fluid, not myocardium.
equipment: Long spinal needle or pericardiocentesis kit
Drainage catheter kit if placing catheter
positioning: Supine or head elevated if tolerated
Expose chest/subxiphoid area widely
Position ultrasound for continuous view
steps: Confirm effusion with tamponade physiology/clinical instability.
Identify safest window with ultrasound.
Prep/drape and anesthetize if time allows.
Advance needle toward fluid pocket while aspirating and visualizing tip when possible.
Aspirate pericardial fluid until hemodynamics improve or flow stops.
If catheter placed, advance wire/catheter with Seldinger control.
Secure catheter and reassess with ultrasound/hemodynamics.
ultrasound: Ultrasound guidance is central: choose the largest accessible pocket with safest path.
Use in-plane visualization when operator skill and window allow.
Re-scan after drainage to assess residual fluid and cardiac filling.
confirmation: Improved blood pressure/perfusion
Improved tachycardia or arrest physiology
Ultrasound decrease in effusion/tamponade findings
Drainage of pericardial fluid rather than blood from chamber
troubleshooting: No aspirate: reassess window, needle depth, clot, or wrong diagnosis.
Bright pulsatile blood: stop and reassess possible chamber puncture.
Recurrent instability: re-scan for reaccumulation, clot, tension pneumothorax, hemorrhage, or obstructive shock alternative.
aftercare: Continuous monitoring and repeat ultrasound.
Leave catheter/drain per local/specialty plan when placed.
Cardiology/cardiothoracic/trauma involvement depending etiology.
Definitive management of cause.
documentation: Pericardiocentesis performed for suspected/confirmed tamponade with hemodynamic compromise.
Consent/emergent rationale documented.
Ultrasound used to identify effusion and procedural window when feasible.
Needle/catheter advanced with aspiration; volume/character of fluid documented.
Hemodynamic response and complications documented.
Consultation and ongoing drainage plan documented.
seniorPearls: The ultrasound diagnosis is not just 'effusion'; it is effusion plus physiology plus patient context.
Pick the safest window, not the most traditional one.
Small amount out with sudden BP improvement is a real win.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify local institutional policy, kit contents, consent requirements, and credentialing expectations.

visualAssets.title: Subxiphoid ultrasound approach
visualAssets.subtitle: Phased array probe in the subxiphoid window angled toward the left shoulder. Pericardial effusion appears as an anechoic stripe surrounding the heart.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Always use ultrasound guidance. Blind pericardiocentesis carries significant risk.

visualAssets.title: Needle entry point
visualAssets.subtitle: 1–2 cm inferior and left of the xiphoid process, needle directed toward the left shoulder at 15–30 degrees from the skin. Aspirate continuously during advance.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Steeper angles risk myocardial puncture. Shallow approach under the costal margin.

shiftMode: Use when unstable bradycardia is not responding or needs bridge therapy.
Keep transcutaneous pacing/pressors running while placing if unstable.
Know the generator: rate, output, sensitivity, battery, connections.
Electrical capture is not enough; confirm mechanical capture with pulse/BP/arterial waveform.
Secure catheter and generator carefully; dislodgement is common.
Do not remove backup pads/pressors until capture is reliable and the catheter is secured.
indications: Unstable bradycardia refractory to medications/transcutaneous pacing
High-grade AV block with instability
Bridge to permanent pacing or definitive management
contraindications: No absolute contraindication in life-threatening bradycardia
Severe agitation may require sedation/airway planning
Infection or thrombus at access site affects site selection
anatomy: Usually placed through central venous access into RV.
Balloon-tipped catheter floats with blood flow toward RV when advanced correctly.
ECG changes and capture help guide placement.
positioning: Supine
Sterile central line position based on chosen access site
Defib pads on patient
steps: Obtain central venous access/sheath.
Connect pacing catheter to generator correctly and test function.
Inflate balloon per catheter instructions.
Advance catheter while watching ECG for ectopy/capture clues.
Set rate/output and advance until capture achieved.
Lower output to find threshold, then set safety margin above threshold.
Confirm mechanical capture with pulse/BP/arterial waveform.
Deflate balloon if appropriate, secure catheter/generator, obtain imaging.
ultrasound: Ultrasound helps obtain venous access and may help assess catheter course/complications.
Fluoroscopy is ideal when available but often not available in ED crash context.
confirmation: Electrical capture on ECG
Mechanical capture with palpable pulse/BP/arterial waveform
Improved perfusion/mental status
Chest x-ray for catheter position and complications
troubleshooting: No capture: increase mA, check connections/generator, reposition catheter, continue transcutaneous pacing/pressors.
Intermittent capture: secure, reposition, increase output margin.
Ventricular ectopy: withdraw/reposition if irritating myocardium.
Hypotension despite capture: reassess rate, shock, medications, and mechanical complications.
aftercare: Continuous monitoring.
Cardiology/ICU admission and permanent pacer planning if indicated.
Repeat capture checks after movement/transport.
Secure generator and catheter to prevent accidental migration.
documentation: Transvenous pacemaker placed for unstable bradycardia/conduction disease.
Consent/emergent rationale documented.
Venous access obtained using documented site/technique.
Pacing catheter advanced and capture achieved at documented settings.
Mechanical capture confirmed clinically/hemodynamically.
Catheter secured; imaging/consultation plan documented.
seniorPearls: The most common fake success is seeing spikes and forgetting to check a pulse.
Keep pads on until the whole setup is stable and secured.
Every movement can lose capture; check after transport.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify local institutional policy, kit contents, consent requirements, and credentialing expectations.

visualAssets.title: TVP capture confirmation
visualAssets.subtitle: Visual slot for electrical vs mechanical capture and catheter depth cues.
visualAssets.caption: Placeholder metadata now; replace assetName with a bundled image when final reviewed artwork is added.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: The image must teach not to accept spikes without capture.

shiftMode: This is an airway procedure wearing a comfort-procedure costume.
Separate proceduralist from sedation/monitoring clinician when possible.
Pre-brief airway risk, hemodynamics, meds, dose plan, and rescue plan.
Use continuous pulse ox, cardiac monitor, frequent BP, and ideally ETCO2.
Have suction, BVM, airway adjuncts, and reversal/rescue meds physically in the room.
Do not give more medication because the room is impatient; dose to patient response.
indications: Painful or anxiety-provoking procedure requiring controlled sedation
Reduction, cardioversion, abscess drainage, chest tube, complex wound care
Facilitation of urgent imaging/procedure when benefits outweigh risk
contraindications: High aspiration risk, difficult airway, severe physiologic instability, or inadequate staffing/equipment are major cautions
Agent-specific contraindications depend on medication and patient physiology
Recent oral intake is contextual, not an automatic absolute contraindication in emergencies
anatomy: Airway patency, respiratory drive, and hemodynamic reserve are the real anatomy here.
Know whether the patient will be supine, lateral, or immobilized during the procedure.
equipment: Cardiac monitor, pulse oximeter, BP cycling
BVM with mask and PEEP valve if available
positioning: Position for airway access and procedure success
Preoxygenate when risk is meaningful
Remove loose dentures/foreign bodies if appropriate
steps: Assess indication, airway risk, fasting/context, comorbidities, allergies, and ASA-style risk.
Obtain consent and perform time-out.
Assign sedation monitor and proceduralist roles.
Prepare airway/rescue equipment before medications.
Administer medication incrementally according to agent and patient physiology.
Continuously monitor ventilation, oxygenation, perfusion, and procedural conditions.
Treat airway obstruction/apnea immediately.
Observe until patient returns to appropriate baseline and discharge/admission criteria met.
confirmation: Adequate procedure conditions
Ventilation maintained by ETCO2/chest rise
Oxygenation maintained
Hemodynamics acceptable
Return to baseline mental status/airway function before discharge
troubleshooting: Apnea: stop dosing, jaw thrust, suction, airway adjunct, BVM/PEEP, prepare intubation.
Hypotension: stop sedative, fluids/pressors, reassess bleeding/sepsis/agent effect.
Emergence/agitation: reduce stimulation, protect patient, consider agent-specific management.
Inadequate sedation: pause procedure, reassess analgesia vs sedation, redose deliberately.
aftercare: Observe until stable and appropriately awake.
Document pre/post assessment, meds/doses/times, monitoring, complications, and recovery status.
Give discharge precautions if outpatient.
documentation: Procedural sedation performed to facilitate documented procedure.
Consent obtained; risks/benefits discussed when feasible.
Patient monitored with cardiac monitor, pulse oximetry, blood pressure, and capnography when available.
Sedation medications/doses/times documented in MAR/procedure record.
Patient tolerated sedation; airway/hemodynamic events and interventions documented.
Recovered to appropriate baseline prior to disposition.
seniorPearls: Sedation fails when the procedure starts before the room is ready.
ETCO2 buys time; pulse ox tells you late.
The safest sedative is the one you know how to rescue from.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify local institutional policy, kit contents, consent requirements, and credentialing expectations.

visualAssets.title: Sedation room setup
visualAssets.subtitle: Visual slot for airway/resuscitation setup before medication delivery.
visualAssets.caption: Placeholder metadata now; replace assetName with a bundled image when final reviewed artwork is added.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Premium version should show the room before drugs go in.

shiftMode: Confirm indication and whether imaging/platelets/anticoagulation review is needed before starting.
Position is the procedure: poor flexion and rotation kill success.
Use L3-L4 or L4-L5; avoid going too high.
Have tubes labeled and opening pressure setup ready before puncture if needed.
If traumatic or no flow, pause and reposition rather than digging.
Document neuro exam and consent before local anesthetic when feasible.
indications: Concern for meningitis/encephalitis when LP indicated
Subarachnoid hemorrhage evaluation after appropriate imaging pathway
Measurement of opening pressure
Diagnostic CSF studies for inflammatory, malignant, or infectious etiologies
contraindications: Signs of elevated ICP/mass lesion requiring imaging first
Coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia or anticoagulation depending context
Overlying infection at puncture site
Unstable patient who cannot tolerate positioning
anatomy: Use line between iliac crests approximating L4 spinous process.
Needle enters midline between spinous processes toward umbilicus.
Conus usually ends above adult L3; use lower lumbar spaces.
equipment: Manometer tubing if opening pressure needed
Extra needles if obese or difficult anatomy
positioning: Lateral decubitus for opening pressure; hips/neck flexed but airway comfortable
Sitting position can improve success but opening pressure is less reliable
Shoulders and hips perpendicular to bed
steps: Review contraindications, consent, and required studies.
Position patient carefully and identify interspace.
Prep/drape sterile field.
Anesthetize skin and deeper tissue.
Insert spinal needle with stylet bevel oriented appropriately.
Advance slowly until pop/loss of resistance; remove stylet to check flow.
Measure opening pressure if indicated in lateral decubitus.
Collect CSF in ordered tubes.
Replace stylet before removing needle; dress site and reassess.
ultrasound: Ultrasound can help identify midline and depth in obese or difficult anatomy but does not replace sterile technique.
confirmation: CSF obtained and sent for ordered studies
Opening pressure documented if measured
Patient neurologic status reassessed
Tube numbers and appearance documented
troubleshooting: No CSF: recheck positioning, level, angle, depth; consider longer needle or ultrasound marking.
Bone repeatedly: withdraw to subcutaneous tissue and redirect cephalad slightly.
Traumatic tap: minimize needle movement; consider another level if needed.
Post-LP headache: counsel, fluids/analgesia, blood patch pathway if persistent/severe.
aftercare: Supine rest per local practice if symptomatic.
Send CSF promptly with correct labels/orders.
Reassess neuro status and puncture site.
Give return precautions for severe headache, fever, weakness, urinary retention, or site issues.
documentation: Lumbar puncture performed for documented indication.
Consent obtained; contraindications reviewed as appropriate.
Patient positioned and sterile technique used.
Needle inserted at documented level; CSF obtained/unsuccessful attempts documented.
Opening pressure documented if measured.
CSF appearance/tubes/studies documented; patient tolerated procedure with complications noted.
seniorPearls: Most failed LPs are positioning failures, not needle failures.
Do not chase bone deeper and deeper; reset your angle.
Label tubes before you are holding CSF and everyone is suddenly excited.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify local institutional policy, kit contents, consent requirements, and credentialing expectations.

visualAssets.title: LP interspace landmarks
visualAssets.subtitle: Tuffier's line (a line connecting the iliac crests) crosses at approximately the L4 spinous process. L3–L4 or L4–L5 interspaces are the target, below the conus medullaris.
visualAssets.caption: Illustration pending clinical review. Use the visual asset workflow before bundling final artwork.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: The conus medullaris typically ends at L1–L2. Do not insert above L3.

visualAssets.title: Needle trajectory
visualAssets.subtitle: Midline approach: needle angled slightly cephalad, passing through skin, subcutaneous tissue, supraspinous ligament, interspinous ligament, ligamentum flavum, then dura. Pop felt at dura entry.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: If bone is hit, withdraw and redirect — do not force through.

shiftMode: Confirm indication, site preference (radial first), and assess collateral flow with ultrasound before committing.
Position wrist in 20-30° extension over a firm roll; tape securely without restricting distal circulation.
Ultrasound preferred: identify radial artery, confirm pulsatility and depth, plan entry point.
Anesthetize even in awake patients — arterial puncture without local anesthetic causes involuntary movement and failed placement.
Advance catheter-over-needle with bevel up at 30-45°; on flashback, flatten and advance catheter. Use Seldinger for deep or small vessels.
Arterial waveform on the transducer is definitive confirmation — a flat or venous tracing means reposition or replace.
Label, zero, and document waveform quality before relying on readings.
indications: Continuous beat-to-beat blood pressure monitoring in hemodynamic instability or vasopressor titration
Frequent arterial blood gas sampling (>4-6 ABGs anticipated)
Non-invasive BP unreliable due to arrhythmia, severe hypotension, obesity, or peripheral vascular disease
High-risk procedures, RSI, or sedation requiring real-time hemodynamic monitoring
contraindications: No identifiable arterial signal at planned site — do not attempt blind
Raynaud phenomenon, limb ischemia, or insufficient collateral flow (relative; assess with ultrasound)
Active infection, burn, or wound overlying the insertion site
Suspected arterial disease, thrombosis, or prior catheterization damage at site
Severe coagulopathy — weigh urgency; compressible sites preferred
anatomy: Radial artery runs along the lateral volar forearm, superficial to the flexor carpi radialis tendon; typically 2-4 mm diameter at 3-8 mm depth.
Dual radial-ulnar supply provides collateral protection; assess with ultrasound if perfusion uncertain.
Femoral offers larger target but higher infection and noncompressible-bleeding risk.
Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial are alternatives when upper extremities are inaccessible.
Artery is pulsatile and non-compressible; distinguish clearly from the adjacent cephalic vein.
equipment: Arterial catheter kit or 20-gauge catheter-over-needle with Seldinger wire
Local anesthetic (1% lidocaine), syringe, and small-gauge needle
Arm board or rolled towel for wrist positioning
Arterial pressure transducer and tubing with NS flush bag pressurized to 300 mmHg
Ultrasound machine with sterile probe cover and sterile gel
Suture or adhesive securement device per preference
positioning: Extend wrist 20-30° over a firm roll (rolled towel or IV bag) with the volar surface facing up.
Tape hand and forearm securely to arm board; prevent movement without restricting distal perfusion.
Abduct arm and position ultrasound screen in direct line of sight before prep.
steps: Confirm indication, check for contraindications, and obtain consent if non-emergent.
Set up pressure transducer and tubing; connect to monitor and zero at the phlebostatic axis.
Position wrist over roll in 20-30° extension; secure with tape.
Perform ultrasound survey: identify artery, confirm pulsatility, estimate depth, and plan entry point.
Prep skin widely with chlorhexidine; apply sterile drape.
Infiltrate skin and subcutaneous tissue with local anesthetic along the planned tract.
With ultrasound guidance, advance needle at 30-45° bevel-up; watch for pulsatile flash.

On flash: for catheter-over-needle, flatten slightly, advance catheter off needle, remove needle; for Seldinger, flatten, pass wire without force, remove needle, thread catheter over wire, remove wire.
Connect arterial tubing; confirm arterial waveform on monitor.
Secure catheter, apply clear dressing, and label with site and date.
Zero transducer at appropriate reference point; document waveform quality.
ultrasound: Short-axis approach: confirm artery is pulsatile and non-compressible; distinguish from adjacent vein.
Long-axis approach: useful for visualizing needle trajectory and catheter advancement.
Confirm wire position in vessel lumen before advancing catheter with Seldinger technique.
Post-placement: assess distal perfusion if any concern about thrombosis or vasospasm.
confirmation: Pulsatile arterial waveform visible on bedside monitor
Bright red flash at time of needle entry
All ports flush smoothly; no resistance
Catheter secured and labeled; transducer zeroed
Flush system continuously pressurized at ~300 mmHg
troubleshooting: Flash without waveform: check transducer connections, tubing, and zero; re-advance catheter slightly or retry Seldinger.
Catheter will not advance over needle: do not force; switch to Seldinger technique or resite.
Dampened waveform: check for clot at catheter tip, kink in tubing, or transducer below heart level.
Loss of signal with movement: improve limb securement and confirm catheter is not kinked.
Hematoma: remove catheter, hold firm pressure ≥5 min (longer if anticoagulated), and check distal perfusion.
complications: Thrombosis with distal ischemia — remove catheter immediately if suspected
aftercare: Remove catheter as soon as hemodynamic monitoring is no longer required.
Hold firm pressure 5-10 minutes after removal (longer if anticoagulated).
Assess distal perfusion and capillary refill after removal.
Change site or remove per institutional infection-prevention policy.
documentation: Arterial line placed at [radial/femoral/dorsalis pedis] for [indication].
Consent obtained when feasible; emergent need documented when not possible.
Ultrasound guidance used; vessel confirmed as artery by pulsatility and non-compressibility.
Catheter advanced via [catheter-over-needle / Seldinger] technique.
Arterial waveform confirmed on monitor; transducer zeroed.
Catheter secured with clear dressing and labeled with date and site.
Distal perfusion intact after placement.
seniorPearls: The wrist roll is often underdone — a firm, secure 20-30° extension dramatically improves first-pass success.
Seldinger technique outperforms catheter-over-needle in patients with deep, small, or difficult vessels.
A persistently dampened waveform is a reason to replace the line, not to accept inaccurate readings.
references: Roberts JR, Custalow CB. Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine. Arterial Catheterization chapter.
Society of Critical Care Medicine and ACEP guidelines on arterial line placement, hemodynamic monitoring, and infection prevention.

visualAssets.title: Radial artery approach
visualAssets.subtitle: Wrist extended 30–45 degrees over a towel roll. Radial artery palpated 1–2 cm proximal to the wrist crease, lateral to the flexor carpi radialis tendon.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Avoid hyperextension — it can occlude the artery.

visualAssets.title: Ultrasound short-axis view
visualAssets.subtitle: Linear probe perpendicular to the radial artery. Small pulsatile structure visible at 2–5 mm depth. Radial nerve and vein are adjacent.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Use minimal probe pressure to avoid compressing the artery flat.

shiftMode: Confirm indication (diagnostic vs. therapeutic) and laterality; verify effusion is free-flowing on ultrasound — not loculated.
Mark the site with the patient in the procedure position; fluid pockets shift significantly when patients change position.
Puncture over the superior margin of the lower rib to avoid the intercostal neurovascular bundle (nerve-artery-vein runs inferior to each rib).
Anesthetize all the way to the pleural surface — the parietal pleura is where the pain is.
For therapeutic: connect drainage before advancing catheter; preventing air entry is critical.
Stop at 1–1.5 L per session unless monitoring closely; cough and chest tightness are early warnings of re-expansion risk.
Post-procedure ultrasound or CXR to confirm adequate drainage and screen for pneumothorax.
indications: Diagnostic evaluation of a new, undiagnosed pleural effusion
Therapeutic relief of respiratory compromise from a large pleural effusion
Evaluation of parapneumonic effusion for empyema, complicated effusion, or effusion requiring drainage
contraindications: Uncooperative patient unable to remain still during procedure (relative; consider procedural sedation)
Uncorrected coagulopathy with significant bleeding risk — weigh urgency and available reversal
Effusion too small or clearly loculated with no safe accessible pocket on ultrasound
Active ipsilateral pneumothorax — decompress first
Known lung adherence to chest wall at the planned site
anatomy: Pleural space lies between parietal and visceral pleura; fluid accumulates in dependent regions (posterior on upright imaging).
Intercostal neurovascular bundle runs in the inferior groove of each rib: nerve, artery, vein (NAV superior-to-inferior); puncture over the superior margin of the lower rib.
Diaphragm rises during expiration and varies with body habitus; ultrasound confirms safe distance before puncture.
Right-sided approach: liver is at risk below the 9th rib posteriorly.
Left-sided approach: spleen is at risk; confirm upper boundary of effusion before each approach.
equipment: Thoracentesis kit (needle, catheter, three-way stopcock, 60 mL syringe)
Local anesthetic (1% lidocaine), syringe, and 22-25 gauge anesthesia needle
Large-bore drainage tubing and vacuum drainage bottles for therapeutic drainage
Specimen collection tubes (LDH, protein, glucose, pH, cell count with differential, culture, cytology as appropriate)
Blood culture bottles for inoculation at bedside (highest yield for SBP/empyema detection)
positioning: Seated and leaning forward over a padded bedside table with arms resting on a pillow — drops the scapula and opens interspaces for posterior access.
Supine with ipsilateral arm raised overhead is acceptable for ventilated or unable-to-sit patients; locate fluid in this position.
Perform ultrasound with the patient in the exact position you will use for the procedure.
steps: Confirm the effusion side, size, and indication; determine diagnostic vs. therapeutic approach.
Obtain consent when feasible; document emergent need if not possible.
Perform real-time ultrasound with patient in procedure position; identify the optimal fluid pocket and mark the entry site.
Prep and drape the posterior-lateral chest at the marked site.
Anesthetize skin, subcutaneous tissue, rib periosteum, and pleural surface with local anesthetic, aspirating to confirm proximity to fluid as you advance.
Insert the procedure needle along the superior margin of the lower rib with continuous aspiration.
On pleural fluid return, lock needle position; for diagnostic aspiration, collect 30-60 mL directly.
For therapeutic: advance catheter over needle or via Seldinger technique, then connect drainage tubing to prevent air entry.
Drain fluid via syringe aspiration or gravity to vacuum bottle; monitor symptoms continuously.
For therapeutic drainage: stop at 1-1.5 L per session; stop earlier if patient develops cough, chest pain, or dyspnea.
Withdraw catheter/needle at end of expiration or with patient performing Valsalva; apply occlusive dressing immediately.
Obtain confirmatory imaging (CXR or lung ultrasound) within 1-2 hours.
ultrasound: Use a low-frequency curvilinear or phased-array probe in the posterior-lateral chest to identify the effusion in coronal plane.
Confirm fluid is anechoic or hypoechoic and free-flowing with respiration — loculations appear as hyperechoic strands.
Measure the largest pocket depth and distance from skin to fluid before marking.
Mark the entry site with the patient in the procedure position; fluid pockets shift with position changes.
Post-procedure: scan for pneumothorax (absent lung sliding ipsilateral) before imaging is complete.
confirmation: Pleural fluid aspirated freely and consistent with expected character (serous, serosanguineous, or purulent)
No sudden air return (would indicate lung puncture or pneumothorax development)
Respiratory symptoms improved for therapeutic indication
Post-procedure lung ultrasound or CXR: lung expanded, no pneumothorax
Specimens labeled and sent promptly
troubleshooting: No fluid on aspiration: confirm with real-time ultrasound — pocket may have shifted; use live guidance for next attempt.
Air return: withdraw immediately; the needle may be in lung parenchyma; assess urgently for pneumothorax.
Bloody return: distinguish hemorrhagic effusion from hemothorax vs. vessel injury — non-clotting blood suggests pleural; rapidly clotting suggests vascular.
Cough or chest tightness after 1 L: stop drainage; may indicate re-expansion risk or pleural irritation; observe and reassess.
Loculated pocket: scan multiple windows; if truly inaccessible, consult interventional radiology or thoracics.
complications: Pneumothorax (5-10% without ultrasound; <1-3% with ultrasound guidance)
aftercare: Monitor O2 saturation, respiratory rate, and breath sounds for ≥1 hour after procedure.
Obtain CXR or lung ultrasound to evaluate for pneumothorax and residual effusion.
Send pleural fluid samples promptly; pH degrades quickly if not capped.
Record total volume drained and fluid character; document in procedure note.
documentation: Thoracentesis performed at [right/left], [interspace], for [indication].
Consent obtained / emergent need documented.
Ultrasound guidance used for site selection and real-time guidance.
[Volume] mL of [serous/serosanguineous/purulent] fluid drained; no air return.
Specimens sent for [LDH, protein, cell count, culture, cytology as ordered].
Post-procedure imaging: [result, e.g., no pneumothorax, residual small effusion].
Patient tolerated procedure without oxygen desaturation, respiratory distress, or significant bleeding.
seniorPearls: Mark the site with the patient in the procedure position — the fluid pocket moves when they lie down and you will miss it.
Most missed pneumothoraces occur in ventilated or obtunded patients who cannot report symptoms; get post-procedure ultrasound routinely.
Stop before 1.5 L unless you have a compelling reason and are monitoring closely; re-expansion pulmonary edema is rare but underappreciated.
references: Light RW. Pleural Diseases, 6th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.
Havelock T et al. Pleural procedures and thoracic ultrasound: British Thoracic Society guideline 2010. Thorax.
ACEP and SCCM clinical guidelines on ultrasound-guided thoracentesis.

visualAssets.title: Thoracentesis entry site
visualAssets.subtitle: Posterior-lateral approach, rib superior margin, neurovascular bundle location, and safe zone.
visualAssets.caption: Placeholder: replace assetName with a bundled image when final reviewed artwork is added.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Visual must make neurovascular bundle avoidance and diaphragm boundary unambiguous.

shiftMode: Confirm indication (diagnostic vs. therapeutic); verify ascites is present and accessible by ultrasound.
Ultrasound marks the largest, safest pocket in the LLQ — avoids bowel, bladder, and inferior epigastric vessels.
Z-track technique: displace skin laterally before needle entry, release after entering peritoneum — reduces post-procedure fluid leak.
For large-volume (>5 L): administer albumin 6-8 g per liter removed to prevent paracentesis-induced circulatory dysfunction.
Do not give albumin for diagnostic or small-volume (<5 L) taps — no evidence of benefit.
Every hospitalized cirrhotic with ascites who decompensates needs a diagnostic tap — you cannot rule out SBP clinically.
Inoculate blood culture bottles at the bedside from the first 10-20 mL drawn — highest diagnostic yield for SBP.
indications: New-onset ascites requiring diagnostic evaluation
Suspected spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in any hospitalized patient with cirrhosis and ascites
Therapeutic large-volume paracentesis for tense or symptomatic ascites causing respiratory compromise or discomfort
Evaluation of ascites in acute decompensated liver disease, malignancy, or unknown etiology
contraindications: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — significant bleeding risk
Surgical abdomen where bowel perforation is possible at all accessible sites
Active infection, wound, or burn overlying all potential insertion sites
Known adhesions or bowel loops adherent to the anterior abdominal wall at the planned site
INR >2.0 or platelets <50,000 are relative contraindications; routine FFP correction is not required but individual bleeding risk applies
anatomy: Standard entry: LLQ 4-6 cm lateral to the midline, midway between umbilicus and anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
RLQ is acceptable alternative if LLQ is unsafe due to prior surgery, ostomy, or other anatomy.
Avoid the midline: risk of diastasis, recanalized umbilical vein, and thin soft tissue.
Inferior epigastric vessels run cephalocaudally approximately 3-5 cm medial to the ASIS — confirm with Doppler ultrasound.
The bladder must be empty; the bowel shifts unpredictably. Ultrasound-guided entry is standard of care.
equipment: Paracentesis kit or 18-gauge needle/catheter with three-way stopcock and 60 mL syringe (diagnostic)
Long catheter or pigtail drain for therapeutic drainage >1 L
Local anesthetic (1% lidocaine), syringe, and small-gauge needle
Large-bore drainage tubing and vacuum collection bottles for therapeutic tap
Specimen collection tubes: cell count with differential, albumin, protein, LDH, glucose, culture
Blood culture bottles for bedside inoculation (best yield for SBP detection)
positioning: Supine with slight left lateral decubitus (left side down) to pool ascites in the LLQ.
Head of bed elevated 30° if patient cannot lie flat due to respiratory compromise.
Empty the bladder before starting — foley or void — to clear the pelvic space.
Scan with the patient in the exact position you will use for the procedure.
steps: Confirm indication and approach (diagnostic vs. therapeutic); verify ascites on ultrasound.
Empty bladder; position patient in slight left lateral decubitus.
Obtain consent when feasible; document emergent need when not possible.
Perform real-time ultrasound; identify optimal pocket in the LLQ or RLQ; mark the entry site.
Prep and drape the marked area.
Anesthetize skin, subcutaneous tissue, and peritoneum with local anesthetic, aspirating as you advance to confirm proximity to fluid.
Using Z-track technique (displace skin 1-2 cm laterally before advancing; release after entering peritoneum), insert needle perpendicular to skin with continuous aspiration.
On peritoneal fluid return, lock needle or advance catheter; note character of fluid.
For diagnostic: collect 30-60 mL; inoculate blood culture bottles at bedside; send labeled specimens.
For therapeutic: connect drainage tubing to catheter; allow gravity or low-pressure vacuum drainage.
For >5 L therapeutic: administer albumin 6-8 g per liter removed concurrent with or immediately after drainage.
Withdraw needle/catheter when drainage complete; apply firm pressure briefly; dress the site with an occlusive bandage.
Monitor BP, HR, and symptoms for 30-60 minutes post-procedure.
ultrasound: Use a curvilinear or low-frequency probe to identify the largest free-flowing fluid pocket in the LLQ.
Confirm depth from skin to fluid and the absence of bowel loops in the intended needle path.
Color Doppler at the planned site identifies the inferior epigastric vessels before puncture.
Mark the entry site with the patient in the procedure position — fluid redistributes when position changes.
Post-procedure: brief scan to assess for residual ascites and any hematoma at the entry site.
confirmation: Peritoneal fluid returns freely on aspiration — straw-colored, turbid, milky, or bloody depending on etiology
Fluid flows freely during therapeutic drainage
No bowel contents (feculent material or food particles) in return
Abdominal distension and discomfort reduce during therapeutic drainage
Albumin replacement administered if >5 L removed
troubleshooting: No fluid return: confirm position with ultrasound; needle may have bypassed the pocket; withdraw and reassess live.
Bowel contents in return: withdraw immediately; monitor for peritonitis signs; most isolated needle punctures of bowel are self-limiting.
Catheter clogs during large-volume tap: reposition patient slightly; flush gently; replace if blocked.
Persistent fluid leak from site post-procedure: hold pressure; Z-track technique reduces this; purse-string suture rarely required.
Neutrophils >250/mm³ with SBP suspicion: treat empirically while awaiting confirmatory culture results.
complications: Paracentesis-induced circulatory dysfunction (large volume without albumin replacement)
Persistent ascites leak requiring surgical repair (rare)
aftercare: For >5 L: confirm albumin replacement administered; reassess BP and HR at 1 hour.
Send specimens promptly; neutrophil count >250 cells/mm³ meets criteria for empiric SBP treatment.
Monitor for abdominal pain, fever, or hematoma for 4-6 hours after the procedure.
Record total volume drained and fluid character in the procedure note.
Educate patient on return precautions: worsening pain, fever, spreading redness, or fluid leak from the site.
documentation: Paracentesis performed at [LLQ/RLQ] for [indication].
Consent obtained / emergent need documented.
Ultrasound guidance used; largest pocket identified at [site].
[Volume] mL of [character] peritoneal fluid removed without complication.
Specimens sent for [cell count, albumin, protein, LDH, culture, cytology as ordered]; blood culture bottles inoculated at bedside.
Albumin [administered at X g per liter / not indicated — volume <5 L].
Patient hemodynamically stable post-procedure; no abdominal rigidity or signs of peritonitis.
seniorPearls: Every hospitalized cirrhotic who decompensates gets a diagnostic tap first — you cannot exclude SBP on exam.
The Z-track technique reduces post-tap ascites leak; teach it and use it consistently.
Albumin replacement for large-volume taps is non-negotiable; paracentesis-induced circulatory dysfunction is underdiagnosed and preventable.
references: EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines on decompensated cirrhosis and management of ascites. J Hepatol. 2018.
Runyon BA. Management of adult patients with ascites due to cirrhosis: Update 2012. Hepatology.
AASLD guidelines on the management of ascites and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.

visualAssets.subtitle: ASIS relationship, inferior epigastric vessel location, umbilicus-to-ASIS midpoint, and danger zones.
