shiftMode: Confirm this is a fluctuant abscess, not cellulitis, a lipoma, or a lymph node — ultrasound identifies occult pockets and depth, avoids dry incisions.
Anesthetize generously before incising — acidic pus inactivates local anesthetic near the core; infiltrate widely around the margin and wait.
The incision must be adequate: span the entire fluctuant cavity and allow complete drainage and loculation breakdown.
Irrigate vigorously with normal saline until return is clear — this step is often rushed.
Loop drain or loose packing prevents premature closure without the pain of tight gauze packing.
Primary closure is contraindicated — leave it open.
Most simple abscesses in immunocompetent patients do not need antibiotics; treat the cavity.
indications: Fluctuant, loculated skin or soft tissue abscess confirmed clinically or by ultrasound
Infected sebaceous cyst, pilonidal abscess, or other skin abscess requiring drainage
Paronychia with subungual extension unresponsive to soaking
contraindications: Tissue not clearly fluctuant and abscess not confirmed — do not incise cellulitis blindly
Hand deep-space infection (flexor tenosynovitis risk) — surgical consultation required
Facial abscess near the dangerous triangle or other high-risk vascular territory — surgical consultation preferred
Signs of necrotizing infection (pain out of proportion, crepitus, skin necrosis, rapid spread) — this is a surgical emergency, not a bedside I&D
Abscess adjacent to neurovascular structures or deep fascia without adequate training and imaging
anatomy: Identify abscess boundaries by palpation and ultrasound; plan incision at point of maximal fluctuance.
Avoid crossing joint lines, tendon sheaths, or neurovascular structures with the incision when possible.
Satellite collections are common — explore for and break down connecting loculations with blunt dissection.
Perirectal and ischiorectal abscesses require colorectal or surgical expertise; do not attempt without proper training.
Avoid end-arterial zones (fingertip, toe, pinna, nasal tip, penis) with epinephrine-containing anesthetic.
equipment: Iris scissors and curved hemostat for blunt dissection
Irrigation syringe (60 mL) and 500-1000 mL normal saline for irrigation
Splash guard or angiocatheter tip for directed irrigation
Loop drain (Penrose or vessel loop) or plain strip gauze packing
Local anesthetic: 1% lidocaine with epinephrine (avoid end-arterial zones) or regional block
Wound cultures if immunocompromise, recurrence, or systemic infection suspected
positioning: Position the abscess for optimal visualization and operator ergonomics — often lateral decubitus for buttock or perianal.
Adequate lighting is critical; use a headlamp or task light for depth visualization.
Pediatric patients may require restraint assistance or procedural sedation for larger abscesses.
steps: Confirm diagnosis with exam and ultrasound; identify all pockets, satellite collections, and the point of maximal fluctuance.
Infiltrate local anesthetic widely around and beneath the abscess margin — not into the cavity. Allow adequate time for onset (2-3 minutes minimum).
Optionally consider regional nerve block for large or anatomically favorable abscesses.
Prep the area; apply sterile drape.
Make a single, adequate linear incision through the abscess dome at maximal fluctuance — it must be long enough to allow complete drainage and instrument access.
Gently express contents; do not squeeze forcefully.
Insert closed hemostat, open along cavity axis, and sweep to break down all loculations — confirm cavity extent.
Irrigate vigorously with normal saline under moderate pressure using a syringe; repeat until return is clear or nearly clear.
Place a loop drain loosely through the cavity (preferred) or insert plain strip gauze loosely — do not pack tightly.
Apply non-adherent dressing over the drain or packing.
Plan follow-up at 48-72 hours for packing change, drain assessment, or wound check.
ultrasound: High-frequency linear probe (10+ MHz) for soft tissue abscess identification.
Confirms hypoechoic or anechoic fluid cavity with posterior acoustic enhancement and internal debris — differentiates from solid mass.
Color Doppler identifies adjacent vessels before incision.
Reveals hidden loculations and satellite pockets that palpation alone misses.
Use ultrasound to guide needle aspiration for depth confirmation before incision in deep or uncertain abscesses.
confirmation: Frank pus drains freely on incision
All loculations broken down and cavity fully explored with instrument
Vigorous irrigation return is clear or near-clear before packing
Loop drain or loose packing placed — not tight, not obliterating the cavity
No evidence of deeper structure involvement (tendon, joint, fascia)
troubleshooting: No pus on incision: reassess with ultrasound — may have incised into cellulitis or missed the pocket; do not extend blindly.
Bleeding during incision: direct pressure; abscess-bed bleeding is usually minor and self-limited.
Inadequate anesthesia despite infiltration: add more anesthetic outside the margin, wait longer; acidic purulent environment inactivates local anesthetic near the core.
Deep pocket inaccessible: ultrasound-guided aspiration to confirm location; surgical consultation if complex anatomy or uncertain.
Recurrence at same site: consider incomplete drainage, retained foreign body, hidradenitis suppurativa, Crohn's disease, or resistant organism.
complications: Abscess recurrence from inadequate drainage or premature closure
Bacteremia (rare with simple abscess; consider antibiotics if systemic signs develop)
Injury to adjacent neurovascular structures if anatomy is not respected
aftercare: Instruct patient on wound care: warm soaks or showering; keep the site clean and moist.
Return visit in 48-72 hours for packing removal or loop drain assessment and wound inspection.
Antibiotics not routinely required for simple abscess in immunocompetent adults; indicated for surrounding cellulitis, systemic infection, immunocompromise, facial location, or MRSA risk factors.
MRSA coverage (TMP-SMX or doxycycline) based on local resistance patterns and risk factors.
Provide clear return precautions: worsening pain, fever, spreading redness, or red streaking.
documentation: Abscess incision and drainage performed at [site and description] for [indication].
Ultrasound performed: [confirmed abscess / depth / satellite collections noted].
Local anesthetic with [agent/concentration] administered; patient reported adequate analgesia.
[Character and estimated volume of pus] evacuated; cavity irrigated with normal saline until clear.
Loculations broken down; [loop drain / loose packing] placed.
Cultures [sent / not sent]; antibiotics [prescribed / not prescribed] with rationale documented.
Patient discharged with wound care instructions and 48-72 hour follow-up arranged.
seniorPearls: Acidic pus inactivates local anesthetic inside the cavity — infiltrate around the margin and wait; never rush into an under-anesthetized abscess.
The incision length is the most common technical mistake: if you cannot freely break down all loculations and irrigate completely, it is not big enough.
Packing is not the therapy — irrigation and complete drainage are. Packing is just a spacer.
references: Roberts JR, Custalow CB. Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine. Abscess Incision and Drainage chapter.
Talan DA et al. Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole versus Placebo for Uncomplicated Skin Abscess. N Engl J Med. 2016;374:823-832.
Singer AJ et al. Loop drainage vs. primary closure after abscess I&D. Acad Emerg Med. 2014.

visualAssets.title: I&D technique overview
visualAssets.subtitle: Incision placement, blunt dissection direction, irrigation setup, and loop drain placement.

visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Visual should clarify incision length adequacy and loculation breakdown — the two most common technical failures.

shiftMode: Confirm you actually need large-bore central access (massive transfusion, rapid volume, or a conduit for a transvenous pacer/PA catheter) — a triple lumen is not interchangeable with an introducer.
Full sterile barrier and ultrasound, exactly as for any central line. The larger dilator is less forgiving, not more.
Confirm the wire is venous before dilating — arterial dilation with an 8.5 Fr sheath is a surgical emergency.
Dilator and sheath advance together as a unit over the wire; keep the wire moving freely and never lose the back end.
Advance with gentle rotation, not force. The skin nick must be adequate or the sheath tip will peel back.
Remove dilator and wire together, then immediately cap or connect the side port and aspirate/flush.
Confirm position and screen for pneumothorax/arterial placement per site and local policy.
indications: Anticipated massive transfusion or rapid large-volume resuscitation
Need for a conduit to float a transvenous pacemaker or pulmonary artery catheter
High-flow access when peripheral large-bore IVs are inadequate or impossible
Hemodynamic instability requiring reliable, high-flow central access
contraindications: Patient refusal when non-emergent
Infection, burn, or significant injury overlying the intended site
Thrombosis or distorted anatomy at the intended vein
Uncorrected severe coagulopathy is a relative risk; weigh against urgency and choose a compressible site
Do not place a large introducer when the clinical need is met by a smaller multi-lumen catheter
anatomy: IJ and femoral are the usual sites; subclavian/axillary carry higher pneumothorax and noncompressible-bleeding risk with a large sheath.
The vein must comfortably accommodate an 8.5 Fr (or larger) sheath — confirm caliber and compressibility on ultrasound.
Distinguish compressible vein from pulsatile, noncompressible artery before puncture; the consequence of error scales with sheath size.
For IJ, note proximity of the carotid and the pleural dome at the base of the neck.
equipment: Introducer/Cordis kit with sheath, integrated dilator, guidewire, needle, and scalpel
Sterile gown, gloves, mask, cap, and large sterile drape
Ultrasound machine with sterile probe cover and sterile gel
Local anesthetic, syringe, and small needle
Sterile saline flush for the side port
Suture or securement device and sterile dressing
Confirmation workflow (chest x-ray) for IJ/subclavian/axillary placement
positioning: IJ: Trendelenburg if tolerated, head slightly turned away, neck not over-rotated.
Femoral: supine, leg slightly abducted and externally rotated if tolerated.
Set the ultrasound screen in your direct line of sight before sterile prep.
Optimize lighting and table height before opening the kit.
steps: Confirm indication for large-bore access and select the site by urgency, anatomy, and bleeding risk.
Ultrasound survey before opening the kit: confirm vein caliber, patency, and trajectory.
Prep and drape with full sterile barrier technique.
Anesthetize skin and deeper tissue along the planned tract.
Cannulate the vein under ultrasound guidance, maintaining needle-tip awareness; confirm venous blood return.
Pass the guidewire smoothly without force; confirm wire is venous with ultrasound when feasible.
Make an adequate skin nick at the wire with the scalpel, controlling the wire at all times.
Advance the assembled dilator-and-sheath over the wire as a unit, using gentle rotation; do not force.
Remove the dilator and wire together while holding the sheath in place; confirm the wire is out intact.
Aspirate and flush the side port; cap or connect infusion.
Secure the sheath and apply a sterile dressing.
Confirm position and assess for complications per site and local policy.
ultrasound: Confirm the vein is large enough for the sheath and compresses fully before puncture.
Use short-axis for vessel ID; long-axis helps track needle and wire if skilled.
Confirm guidewire within the vein before dilating — this is the highest-stakes confirmation given the dilator size.
For IJ, check lung sliding afterward as a rapid pneumothorax screen, but follow local confirmation standards.
confirmation: Side port aspirates and flushes easily
Guidewire and dilator removed intact
Sheath secured with sterile dressing
Appropriate imaging/confirmation obtained for the site
No evidence of arterial placement, pneumothorax, hematoma, or malposition
troubleshooting: Wire will not pass: stop, do not force; recheck needle position and angle, aspirate, use ultrasound, or repuncture.
Sheath tip peels back or kinks on advancement: the skin/tract nick is too small — withdraw, enlarge the nick, and re-advance over a controlled wire.
Bright pulsatile blood or arterial waveform before dilation: stop; do not dilate; manage as arterial puncture.
Brisk bleeding around the sheath: apply pressure, assess coagulopathy, and confirm intraluminal placement.
Unstable after placement: consider pneumothorax, bleeding, arrhythmia, air embolism, or progression of underlying shock.
complications: Arterial cannulation with a large-bore sheath (surgical emergency)
Pneumothorax or hemothorax, especially with IJ/subclavian/axillary approaches
Hematoma or significant bleeding given the large tract
Air embolism through an uncapped large-bore sheath
Arrhythmia from wire or catheter irritation
aftercare: Keep the side port capped or connected at all times to prevent air embolism.
Label the sheath and date the dressing per local policy.
Review confirmation imaging before high-risk use if required by site/policy.
Remove the introducer as soon as large-bore access is no longer required.
Reassess the site for bleeding or hematoma after placement.
documentation: Introducer sheath (Cordis) placed for documented large-bore access indication.
Consent obtained when feasible; emergent need documented if not possible.
Site and side confirmed; full sterile barrier precautions used.
Ultrasound guidance used to identify the vein and guide cannulation.
Guidewire passed without resistance and removed intact with the dilator.
Side port aspirated and flushed; sheath secured with sterile dressing.
Placement confirmation and immediate complication assessment documented.
seniorPearls: The skin nick is the step most people underdo; an 8.5 Fr sheath needs a real nick or the tip accordions on the way in.
Dilation is irreversible — if you are not certain the wire is venous, you are not ready to dilate.
An uncapped large-bore sheath is an open conduit for air; cap or connect it the moment the dilator and wire come out.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, central venous access and introducer sheath technique.
Major critical care and trauma resuscitation texts on large-bore venous access.
Institutional central line bundle and ultrasound-guided vascular access policies; verify local requirements.

visualAssets.title: Dilator-sheath assembly
visualAssets.subtitle: Assembled dilator and sheath advancing over the wire, skin nick, and side-port orientation.
visualAssets.caption: Placeholder: replace assetName with a bundled image when final reviewed artwork is added.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Visual must make wire control and adequate skin nick obvious — the two failure points for large sheaths.

shiftMode: Confirm the need is dialysis-level flow (urgent HD, CRRT, or plasmapheresis) — a triple lumen will not deliver it.
Site choice matters: right IJ is preferred for function and lower complication; avoid subclavian to preserve future fistula options.
Full sterile barrier and ultrasound, identical discipline to any central line.
Confirm the wire is venous before dilating; dialysis catheters use serial dilators and a large stiff catheter.
Dilate sequentially without force; the tract must be developed enough to pass the catheter but not over-dilated.
Both lumens (arterial and venous ports) must aspirate and flush; lock with the prescribed catheter lock volume.
Confirm tip position and screen for complications before the line is used for dialysis.
indications: Urgent or emergent hemodialysis (hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, fluid overload, uremia, certain toxic ingestions)
Continuous renal replacement therapy in the critically ill
Plasmapheresis or therapeutic apheresis requiring high blood flow
Bridge access while definitive dialysis access is established
contraindications: Patient refusal when non-emergent
Infection or burn over the intended site
Thrombosis or distorted anatomy at the intended vein
Subclavian approach is relatively contraindicated in candidates for future AV fistula (stenosis risk)
Uncorrected severe coagulopathy is a relative risk; weigh urgency and choose a compressible site
anatomy: Right IJ offers the straightest path to the SVC/RA junction and is the preferred site for catheter function.
Femoral is acceptable for short-term or emergent access, with higher infection risk and flow limitations.
Avoid subclavian when possible to preserve the vasculature for future arteriovenous access.
The vein must accommodate a large, stiff dual-lumen catheter — confirm caliber and compressibility on ultrasound.
equipment: Dialysis catheter kit (dual-lumen catheter, guidewire, needle, scalpel, serial dilators)
Sterile gown, gloves, mask, cap, and large sterile drape
Ultrasound machine with sterile probe cover and sterile gel
Local anesthetic, syringe, and small needle
Heparin or citrate catheter lock solution per local protocol
Sterile saline flushes for both lumens
Confirmation workflow (chest x-ray) for IJ/subclavian placement
positioning: IJ: Trendelenburg if tolerated, head slightly turned away, neck not over-rotated.
Femoral: supine, leg slightly abducted and externally rotated if tolerated.
Place the ultrasound screen in your line of sight before sterile prep.
Optimize lighting and table height before opening the kit.
steps: Confirm dialysis-level indication and select the site, preferring right IJ when feasible.
Ultrasound survey before opening the kit: confirm vein caliber, patency, and trajectory.
Prep and drape with full sterile barrier technique.
Anesthetize skin and deeper tissue along the planned tract.
Cannulate the vein under ultrasound guidance with needle-tip awareness; confirm venous blood return.
Pass the guidewire smoothly without force; confirm venous position with ultrasound when feasible.
Make an adequate skin nick at the wire, controlling the wire at all times.
Pass serial dilators over the wire to develop the tract; advance gently with rotation, never forcing.
Advance the dual-lumen catheter over the wire to the planned depth for the site.
Remove the wire while holding the catheter; confirm the wire is out intact.
Aspirate and flush both lumens; lock each with the prescribed lock solution and volume.
Secure the catheter, apply a sterile dressing, and confirm tip position before dialysis use.
ultrasound: Confirm the vein is large enough and compresses fully before puncture.
Use short-axis for vessel ID; long-axis to track the needle and wire if skilled.
Confirm guidewire within the vein before serial dilation.
For IJ, check lung sliding afterward as a rapid pneumothorax screen, but follow local confirmation standards.
confirmation: Both arterial and venous lumens aspirate and flush easily
Guidewire and dilators removed intact
Catheter locked with the prescribed solution and volume
Tip position confirmed by imaging before dialysis use (IJ/subclavian)
No evidence of arterial placement, pneumothorax, hematoma, or malposition
troubleshooting: Wire will not pass: stop, do not force; recheck needle position and angle, aspirate, use ultrasound, or repuncture.
Catheter will not advance despite dilation: the tract is under-developed or the wire has kinked; re-dilate over a controlled wire and recheck.
Bright pulsatile blood or arterial waveform before dilation: stop; do not dilate; manage as arterial puncture.
Poor flow from one lumen after placement: reposition the patient, confirm depth and tip position, and avoid forcing against resistance.
Unstable after placement: consider pneumothorax, bleeding, arrhythmia, air embolism, or progression of underlying illness.
complications: Arterial cannulation with a large-bore catheter (surgical emergency)
Pneumothorax or hemothorax, especially with IJ/subclavian approaches
Hematoma or significant bleeding from the large tract
Catheter dysfunction or inadequate dialysis flow
Central vein stenosis (notably with subclavian placement)
Air embolism, guidewire loss, arrhythmia, infection, or thrombosis
aftercare: Lock both lumens with the prescribed solution; label volumes so they are withdrawn before next use.
Reserve the catheter for dialysis use per local policy to reduce infection and dysfunction.
Confirm tip position on imaging before first dialysis run for IJ/subclavian lines.
Remove the catheter as soon as dialysis access is no longer required.
Reassess the site for bleeding, hematoma, or signs of infection.
documentation: Dialysis catheter (dual-lumen) placed for documented renal-replacement indication.
Consent obtained when feasible; emergent need documented if not possible.
Site and side confirmed; full sterile barrier precautions used.
Ultrasound guidance used to identify the vein and guide cannulation.
Guidewire passed without resistance; serial dilation performed; wire removed intact.
Both lumens aspirated, flushed, and locked with the prescribed solution and volume.
Tip position confirmation and immediate complication assessment documented.
seniorPearls: Right IJ to the cavoatrial junction is the functional sweet spot; femoral works in a pinch but flows and infection rates are worse.
Avoid the subclavian in anyone who may ever need a fistula — central stenosis can cost them future access.
If a lumen will not aspirate, do not flush it under pressure; reposition and reconfirm tip position first.
references: KDOQI clinical practice guidelines for vascular access.
Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, central venous and dialysis catheter access.
Institutional dialysis catheter and central line bundle policies; verify local lock-solution protocol.

visualAssets.title: Catheter tip position
visualAssets.subtitle: Right IJ trajectory to the cavoatrial junction and dual-lumen orientation.
visualAssets.caption: Placeholder: replace assetName with a bundled image when final reviewed artwork is added.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Visual must make safe tip position and site preference clear to avoid malposition and stenosis.

shiftMode: Reserve for difficult access; do not default to it when a visible/palpable vein is available.
Pick a target 0.3-1.5 cm deep with a straight segment; deeper than the catheter is too short to stay in.
Use an adequately long catheter (4.8 cm or longer) — at least one third of the catheter should sit inside the vein, or it will infiltrate.
Avoid the brachial artery and median nerve in the medial upper arm; confirm the structure is a compressible vein.
Track the needle tip, not just tissue movement, in short or long axis as you advance.
Confirm flash, then lower the angle and advance several more millimeters before threading the catheter.
Confirm intraluminal position by flushing while imaging; watch for tissue bulging that signals infiltration.
indications: Difficult intravenous access where surface veins are unavailable or repeatedly fail
Need for reliable peripheral access before escalating to central access
Patients requiring contrast, medications, or resuscitation without an existing line
contraindications: An easily visible or palpable suitable vein (use standard technique first)
Cellulitis, burn, or wound over the intended site
Need for high-flow large-bore central access or vesicant/irritant infusions that warrant central access
Extremity that should be avoided (e.g., AV fistula side, mastectomy/lymphedema side per policy)
anatomy: Common forearm and upper-arm targets include the basilic, brachial, and cephalic veins.
In the medial upper arm, the brachial veins flank the brachial artery and lie near the median nerve — identify all three before puncture.
Veins are compressible and lack a pulsatile, thick wall; arteries are pulsatile and noncompressible.
Deeper veins need a longer catheter so that enough length remains seated within the lumen.
equipment: Ultrasound machine with high-frequency linear probe
Long peripheral IV catheter (4.8 cm / 1.88 in or longer)
Sterile or single-use probe cover and gel per local policy
positioning: Position the arm on a flat, supported surface with the site facing up.
Apply a tourniquet proximally to distend the target vein.
Place the ultrasound screen directly in your line of sight beyond the puncture site.
Sit or stand so your hand, the probe, and the screen form a comfortable straight line.
steps: Survey the extremity with ultrasound; select a compressible vein 0.3-1.5 cm deep with a straight segment.
Distinguish the vein from adjacent artery (pulsatile, noncompressible) and nerve.
Clean the skin and prepare a long catheter and flush.
Center the vein on the screen in short axis (or align it in long axis if skilled).
Insert the catheter at roughly 30-45 degrees, advancing while keeping the needle tip in view.
On flash, lower the angle and advance 2-4 mm further so the catheter tip — not just the needle — is intraluminal.
Thread the catheter off the needle into the vein while stabilizing the hub.
Release the tourniquet, withdraw the needle into the safety device, and attach the extension set.
Aspirate and flush while imaging; confirm intraluminal flow without tissue bulging.
Secure with a clear dressing and label per policy.
ultrasound: Short-axis is easiest for centering; long-axis confirms the catheter lies within the lumen.
Compress to confirm the target is a vein, not an artery, before puncture.
Keep the needle tip in view at all times; advance the probe ahead to relocate a lost tip.
After threading, image the catheter within the vein and watch the flush for intraluminal turbulence rather than tissue expansion.
confirmation: Blood flash and smooth catheter threading
Catheter visualized within the vein lumen on ultrasound
Flushes easily without resistance or tissue bulging
No pulsatile bleeding (would suggest arterial puncture)
Secured with a clear dressing and dated
troubleshooting: Flash but catheter will not thread: the tip is against a wall or only the needle entered — lower the angle, advance slightly, and re-confirm tip position.
Lost needle tip: stop advancing; slide the probe forward to find the tip before moving the needle.
Vein rolls or collapses: reduce probe pressure, reposition, or choose a better-supported segment.
Swelling or resistance on flush: infiltration — stop, remove, hold pressure, and select a new site.
Bright pulsatile blood: arterial puncture — remove, hold firm pressure, and reassess.
complications: Infiltration or extravasation, especially with short catheters in deep veins
Nerve irritation or injury near the brachial bundle
Infection (lower than central access but not zero)
aftercare: Reassess the site for infiltration before and during infusions, particularly with deeper placements.
Document catheter length and depth so the next clinician understands the access.
Replace per local dwell-time policy and remove when no longer needed.
Educate the patient to report swelling, pain, or leakage at the site.
documentation: Ultrasound-guided peripheral IV placed for difficult access.
Vein identified as compressible and non-pulsatile; adjacent artery/nerve noted and avoided.
Long catheter used; intraluminal position confirmed by ultrasound and flush.
Site secured with a clear dressing and labeled.
No immediate evidence of arterial puncture, infiltration, or nerve injury.
seniorPearls: Catheter length is the difference between a line that lasts and one that infiltrates in an hour — match length to depth, not habit.
If you cannot keep the needle tip on screen, you are advancing blind; fan or slide the probe and find it before moving.
The medial upper arm is rich in arteries and nerves; one extra moment confirming the structure is a vein prevents the worst complications.
references: ACEP policy and emergency ultrasound guidelines on ultrasound-guided vascular access.
Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, peripheral venous access.
Society and institutional ultrasound-guided peripheral access protocols; verify local catheter and dwell-time policy.

visualAssets.title: Short-axis probe positioning
visualAssets.subtitle: High-frequency linear probe perpendicular to the target vein. Vein appears as a round anechoic compressible structure. Artery is pulsatile and non-compressible.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Compress to distinguish vein from artery before needle entry.

visualAssets.title: Needle tip visualization
visualAssets.subtitle: Track the needle tip as a hyperechoic dot entering the vein lumen. Tenting of the anterior wall confirms contact. Flash in the catheter hub confirms intraluminal position.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Always visualize the needle tip, not just the shaft. Shaft visualization can be deceptive about true tip location.

shiftMode: This is a vision-saving, time-critical procedure — minutes matter; do not wait for ophthalmology or imaging if the diagnosis is clear.
Diagnose clinically: acute orbital compartment syndrome with a tense, proptotic eye, decreased vision/APD, raised intraocular pressure, and pain after trauma or retrobulbar hemorrhage.
The goal is to release the inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon (cantholysis) — the canthotomy incision alone is not enough.
Anesthetize and clamp the lateral canthus to reduce bleeding, then cut horizontally to the orbital rim.
Strum for the inferior crus and cut it; the lower lid should swing free and the globe should soften.
Reassess intraocular pressure and proptosis; if still tense, release the superior crus as well.
Do not let lack of an ophthalmologist delay the release in a clear orbital compartment syndrome.
indications: Acute orbital compartment syndrome (commonly from retrobulbar hemorrhage) with vision threat
Markedly raised intraocular pressure with a tense, proptotic globe and an afferent pupillary defect
Progressive vision loss with orbital tension after facial/orbital trauma or surgery
contraindications: Suspected globe rupture — manipulating the orbit can extrude intraocular contents; avoid and get emergent ophthalmology
No evidence of orbital compartment syndrome (the procedure treats a pressure problem, not all eye injuries)
An alternative, immediately treatable cause of the findings that does not involve orbital pressure
anatomy: The lateral canthal tendon anchors the eyelids to the orbital rim and has a superior and an inferior crus.
Cantholysis of the inferior crus releases the lower lid and decompresses the orbit; both crura may need release.
The orbital septum and retrobulbar space hold the hemorrhage that raises pressure on the optic nerve and its blood supply.
The lacrimal apparatus sits medially; the lateral approach avoids it. Aim toward the lateral orbital rim, away from the globe.
equipment: Local anesthetic with epinephrine, syringe, and small needle
Gauze and saline for hemostasis and visualization
Tonometer to measure intraocular pressure if available
positioning: Patient supine with the head stabilized and the affected eye fully accessible.
Optimize lighting directly on the lateral canthus.
Have an assistant help retract the lids and manage bleeding if available.
Position yourself at the side of the affected eye for a horizontal lateral approach.
steps: Confirm orbital compartment syndrome clinically and exclude obvious globe rupture.
Infiltrate local anesthetic with epinephrine into the lateral canthus toward the lateral orbital rim.
Clamp the lateral canthus horizontally with a hemostat for 1-2 minutes to crush tissue and reduce bleeding.
Remove the hemostat and make a horizontal canthotomy incision through the crushed tissue to the lateral orbital rim (about 1-2 cm).
Pull the lower lid outward and downward with forceps to put the inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon on tension.
Strum and identify the taut inferior crus, then cut it (inferior cantholysis); the lower lid should release and the globe soften.
Reassess intraocular pressure, proptosis, and tension.
If the orbit remains tense, release the superior crus (superior cantholysis) and reassess.
Control bleeding with gentle pressure and arrange emergent ophthalmology evaluation.
confirmation: Lower lid swings free after inferior cantholysis
Globe is visibly softer and proptosis reduced
Measured intraocular pressure has fallen toward normal if a tonometer is available
Improvement (or halted deterioration) in vision and pupillary findings
Hemostasis achieved at the canthotomy site
troubleshooting: No softening after the incision: the tendon was not released — the canthotomy cut alone is insufficient; perform the cantholysis.
Still tense after inferior cantholysis: release the superior crus as well.
Cannot identify the crus: pull the lid on tension and strum for the taut band toward the orbital rim before cutting.
Brisk bleeding obscuring the field: apply gentle pressure and use the pre-incision hemostat clamp to limit it.
Suspect globe rupture mid-procedure: stop manipulating the globe and get emergent ophthalmology.
complications: Iatrogenic globe injury if the cut strays toward the globe
Incomplete decompression from canthotomy without cantholysis
Scarring or lid malposition after repair
Failure to restore vision if decompression is delayed
aftercare: Arrange emergent ophthalmology evaluation and definitive management.
Continue measures to lower intraocular pressure per ophthalmology guidance.
Serially reassess vision, pupil, and orbital tension until specialist care assumes the patient.
Document the time of decompression relative to symptom onset.
documentation: Lateral canthotomy and inferior (± superior) cantholysis performed for acute orbital compartment syndrome.
Clinical findings documented: proptosis, raised intraocular pressure, vision change, and afferent pupillary defect.
Globe rupture not suspected prior to the procedure.
Inferior crus released with resultant lid release and globe softening; superior crus released if needed.
Post-procedure intraocular pressure, vision, and pupillary findings recorded.
Emergent ophthalmology consultation arranged; time from onset to decompression documented.
seniorPearls: The canthotomy is just the approach — the cantholysis is the treatment; cutting skin without releasing the crus does nothing for the pressure.
This is one of the few true 'cut now, consult after' ophthalmic emergencies; sight is lost in tens of minutes of optic nerve ischemia.
If the globe might be ruptured, stop — decompression can turn a salvageable eye into an extruded one.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, lateral canthotomy and cantholysis.
Ophthalmology and emergency medicine references on orbital compartment syndrome and retrobulbar hemorrhage.
Institutional ophthalmic emergency protocols; verify local consultation pathway.

visualAssets.subtitle: Lateral canthal tendon crura, canthotomy incision line, and cantholysis target at the orbital rim.

shiftMode: Assess the wound for foreign body, tendon/nerve/vessel injury, joint involvement, and contamination before closing anything.
Check distal neurovascular and motor function and document it before infiltrating anesthetic.
Anesthetize, then explore through the full depth and range of motion — injuries hide off the resting position.
Irrigate copiously with normal saline or potable tap water; pressure and volume matter more than additives.
Choose closure: primary now, delayed primary for high-contamination/older wounds, or referral for complex/specialty areas.
Approximate without tension; evert edges; match suture size to location and remove on schedule.
Update tetanus and document wound, exploration, repair, and aftercare.
indications: Acute traumatic laceration amenable to primary closure
Wound where closure improves hemostasis, function, or cosmesis
Clean or cleanable wound within an appropriate time window for the location
contraindications: Signs of established infection (close only after treatment, or use delayed primary closure)
Heavily contaminated or bite wounds in high-risk locations (consider delayed closure or specialty input)
Wounds with retained foreign material not yet removed
Injuries needing operative or specialist repair (tendon, nerve, artery, open fracture, complex facial/eyelid)
Wounds beyond a safe time window for the body region and contamination level
anatomy: Know the underlying structures at the site: tendons, nerves, vessels, ducts, and joints near the wound.
Facial landmarks (vermilion border, eyelid margins, eyebrow) require precise alignment and often specialty care.
Skin tension lines guide orientation and influence scarring.
Depth determines whether deep dermal sutures are needed to close dead space and reduce tension.
equipment: Local anesthetic (lidocaine with or without epinephrine, avoiding epinephrine in end-arterial fields per policy)
Syringe and small-gauge needle for infiltration
Irrigation: normal saline or potable water, large syringe with splash guard
Suture material appropriate to location (e.g., 6-0 face, 4-0/5-0 extremities, deeper absorbable for dermis)
Needle driver, toothed forceps, suture scissors, and a sterile suture tray
Wound dressing and supplies for tetanus prophylaxis
positioning: Position the patient and the wound for direct visualization and ergonomic access.
Optimize lighting; use a headlamp or task light for deep or angled wounds.
Support the limb or area so the patient can hold still during repair.
Examine the wound through its full range of motion to detect deep injuries.
steps: Obtain history including mechanism, timing, contamination, and tetanus status.
Document distal neurovascular and motor function before anesthesia.
Infiltrate local anesthetic and confirm adequate analgesia.
Explore the full wound depth and range of motion for foreign body or deep-structure injury.
Irrigate copiously with saline or potable water under moderate pressure.
Achieve hemostasis and debride devitalized tissue conservatively.
Place deep dermal absorbable sutures if needed to reduce tension and close dead space.
Approximate the skin edges with everting interrupted sutures, matching size and spacing to the location.
Confirm edge apposition and eversion without tension or gaping.
Apply dressing, update tetanus prophylaxis, and give wound-care and suture-removal instructions.
ultrasound: Ultrasound can help localize radiolucent foreign bodies (wood, plastic) not seen on x-ray.
Useful to confirm or exclude retained material before closure when suspicion is high.
Can assess depth and proximity to vessels or tendons in selected wounds.
confirmation: Wound explored and free of foreign body and unrepaired deep injury
Adequate irrigation performed
Edges approximated and everted without tension
Hemostasis achieved
Tetanus status addressed and aftercare instructions given
troubleshooting: Persistent bleeding: direct pressure, identify and control the source; avoid blind deep ties near nerves.
Edges will not approximate without tension: place deep dermal sutures or consider undermining; reassess closure choice.
Inadequate anesthesia: supplement infiltration or use a regional/field block; buffer lidocaine to reduce sting.
Wound edge necrosis or contamination concern: consider delayed primary closure instead of forcing primary repair.
Suspected tendon/nerve injury found on exploration: do not close over it blindly; involve the appropriate specialist.
aftercare: Keep the wound clean and dry per local guidance; instruct on signs of infection.
Provide a suture-removal timeline appropriate to the body region.
Update tetanus prophylaxis based on wound and immunization status.
Arrange follow-up or wound check for higher-risk wounds and consider antibiotics only when indicated.
documentation: Laceration repaired at [site]; mechanism and timing recorded.
Pre-repair distal neurovascular and motor exam documented as intact.
Wound explored; no retained foreign body or deep-structure injury identified (or findings noted).
Irrigation performed; closure type and suture material/size documented.
Tetanus prophylaxis addressed.
Aftercare and suture-removal instructions provided.
seniorPearls: The repair is only as good as the exploration — most missed tendon and foreign-body injuries are closed over, not unfindable.
Examine the wound in the position of injury, not the position of rest, or you will miss tendon lacerations that retracted.
Eversion and tension control determine the scar far more than how fancy the stitch is.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, methods of wound closure.
Emergency medicine references on wound irrigation, closure timing, and tetanus prophylaxis.
Institutional wound-care and antibiotic-prophylaxis policies; verify local guidance.

shiftMode: Confirm anterior dislocation and document neurovascular status — especially axillary nerve sensation over the deltoid — before and after.
Get pre-reduction imaging unless the presentation is clearly recurrent and atraumatic per local practice.
Optimize analgesia/relaxation: intra-articular block, procedural sedation, or a gentle technique the patient can tolerate.
Choose a low-force technique first (scapular manipulation, Cunningham, external rotation) before high-traction methods.
Reduce slowly and steadily; pain and muscle spasm fight speed, not patience.
Confirm reduction clinically and with post-reduction imaging; recheck neurovascular status.
Immobilize and arrange orthopedic follow-up.
indications: Acute anterior glenohumeral dislocation
Confirmed dislocation requiring reduction to restore joint position and relieve neurovascular compromise
Recurrent dislocation appropriate for ED reduction
contraindications: Associated displaced fracture that warrants orthopedic reduction (e.g., certain surgical-neck fractures)
Neurovascular compromise needing urgent surgical evaluation
Posterior or inferior dislocation requiring a different, specific approach
Open dislocation or signs of significant associated injury
Inability to safely provide analgesia/sedation when required
anatomy: The glenohumeral joint trades stability for mobility; the humeral head sits anterior/inferior in the common dislocation.
The axillary nerve wraps the surgical neck of the humerus and is the most commonly injured nerve — test deltoid sensation and function.
The brachial plexus and axillary vessels lie medially; assess distal pulses and perfusion.
Associated injuries include Hill-Sachs and Bankart lesions and greater tuberosity fractures.
equipment: Stretcher or bed allowing the chosen technique and patient positioning
Monitoring and supplies for procedural sedation if used (pulse oximetry, capnography, oxygen, airway kit)
Intra-articular block supplies (local anesthetic, needle) if used
A sheet for counter-traction if a traction technique is chosen
Sling or shoulder immobilizer for post-reduction
Imaging access for pre- and post-reduction films
positioning: Scapular manipulation: patient prone or seated, affected arm hanging with gentle traction.
Cunningham/external rotation: patient seated upright, elbow adducted to the side.
Traction-counter-traction: patient supine with a sheet around the torso for counter-traction.
Choose the position that matches your technique and the patient's tolerance and comfort.
steps: Confirm the diagnosis and document a detailed neurovascular exam including axillary nerve function.
Obtain pre-reduction imaging unless clearly indicated otherwise by local protocol.
Provide analgesia/relaxation: intra-articular block, procedural sedation, or a sedation-sparing gentle technique.
Select a first-line low-force technique (scapular manipulation, Cunningham, or external rotation).
Apply slow, steady force appropriate to the technique, allowing muscle relaxation over time.
Feel or observe for the humeral head relocating into the glenoid.
If unsuccessful, optimize analgesia and try an alternative technique rather than escalating force abruptly.
Confirm reduction clinically (restored contour and motion) and recheck neurovascular status.
Obtain post-reduction imaging to confirm position and assess for fracture.
Immobilize in a sling/immobilizer and arrange orthopedic follow-up.
ultrasound: Bedside ultrasound (posterior approach) can confirm dislocation and successful reduction in real time.
Useful to reduce repeat radiographs and to guide intra-articular block placement.
Can assist confirming relocation before formal post-reduction films.
confirmation: Palpable/visible relocation of the humeral head with restored shoulder contour
Improved range of motion and reduced pain after reduction
Intact post-reduction neurovascular exam (including axillary nerve)
Post-reduction imaging confirms congruent joint and no new fracture
Shoulder immobilized and follow-up arranged
troubleshooting: Reduction failing: ensure adequate analgesia/relaxation; muscle spasm is the usual obstacle — add an intra-articular block or sedation and switch technique.
Persistent inability after multiple attempts: reassess for fracture or alternate dislocation type and involve orthopedics.
New neurologic deficit after reduction: document and arrange urgent orthopedic/neuro evaluation.
Suspected associated fracture on pre-reduction film: weigh technique choice and consult orthopedics.
Severe pain limiting any technique: optimize analgesia before continuing rather than forcing traction.
complications: Rotator cuff injury, especially in older patients
Sedation-related complications when procedural sedation is used
aftercare: Immobilize in a sling or shoulder immobilizer per orthopedic guidance.
Provide analgesia and instructions to avoid provocative positions.
Arrange orthopedic follow-up; first-time dislocations often need outpatient assessment.
Counsel on return precautions: numbness, weakness, worsening pain, or recurrent dislocation.
documentation: Anterior shoulder dislocation confirmed; pre-reduction neurovascular exam (including axillary nerve) documented.
Analgesia/sedation method recorded.
Reduction technique(s) used and number of attempts documented.
Reduction confirmed clinically and on post-reduction imaging.
Post-reduction neurovascular exam documented as intact (or deficits noted).
Immobilization applied and orthopedic follow-up arranged.
seniorPearls: Most failed reductions are really failed analgesia — relax the patient and the shoulder usually follows.
Always test and document the axillary nerve before and after; an unrecorded pre-reduction deficit becomes 'your' deficit.
Lead with low-force techniques; high-traction methods add fracture and soft-tissue risk for little benefit in cooperative patients.
references: Roberts and Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine, management of shoulder dislocations.
Emergency medicine and orthopedic references on anterior glenohumeral reduction techniques.
Institutional protocols on procedural sedation and intra-articular blocks; verify local practice.

visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Visual should make the low-force technique and traction direction clear.

shiftMode: Most useful for hip and proximal femur fractures: opioid-sparing analgesia that helps frail and elderly patients.
Confirm no allergy, no infection over the site, and document distal neurovascular function before the block.
Use ultrasound; deposit local anesthetic beneath the fascia iliaca, lateral to the femoral artery and nerve, not into them.
Calculate a weight-based maximum local anesthetic dose and stay under it — these are large-volume blocks.
Aspirate before and during injection to avoid intravascular delivery; watch for LAST.
Confirm spread under the fascia (a lens of fluid lifting the fascia) rather than within muscle.
Have LAST rescue (lipid emulsion) and resuscitation equipment available before you start.
indications: Analgesia for hip fracture (femoral neck, intertrochanteric) and proximal femur fractures
Femoral shaft fracture pain control
Opioid-sparing analgesia in elderly or frail patients where systemic opioids carry higher risk
contraindications: Patient refusal
Allergy to local anesthetic
Infection or wound over the injection site
Anticoagulation/coagulopathy as a relative risk per local policy
Pre-existing femoral nerve deficit that must be documented (and may warrant caution)
anatomy: The fascia iliaca overlies the iliacus and psoas; the femoral nerve runs deep to it.
Target is the compartment beneath the fascia iliaca, lateral to the femoral artery and nerve.
Landmarks: the femoral artery medially and the anterior superior iliac spine laterally; the injection point is lateral to the artery.
Local anesthetic spreads under the fascia to reach the femoral and lateral femoral cutaneous nerves.
equipment: Ultrasound machine with linear (or curvilinear for larger patients) probe and sterile cover
Block needle (e.g., short-bevel or echogenic) and extension tubing
Local anesthetic in the calculated weight-based volume and concentration
Skin antiseptic, sterile gloves, and drape
Lipid emulsion (Intralipid) and full resuscitation equipment for LAST
positioning: Patient supine with the leg slightly externally rotated and the groin exposed.
Place the ultrasound screen in your direct line of sight across the patient.
Clean and drape the area lateral to the femoral pulse.
Identify the femoral artery, nerve, and the fascia iliaca before needling.
steps: Confirm indication, exclude contraindications, and document distal neurovascular function.
Attach monitoring and ensure LAST rescue equipment is immediately available.
Calculate and draw up a weight-based safe dose of local anesthetic.
Prep and drape; scan to identify the femoral artery, femoral nerve, iliacus, and the overlying fascia iliaca.
Choose an entry point lateral to the femoral nerve and artery.
Advance the needle in-plane under ultrasound toward the plane beneath the fascia iliaca.
Confirm tip position with a small test injection that lifts the fascia (hydrodissection), not muscle.
Aspirate, then inject incrementally with repeated aspiration, watching spread under the fascia.
Observe spread tracking medially and laterally beneath the fascia.
Monitor for LAST and reassess analgesia and distal neurovascular status after the block.
ultrasound: Identify the femoral artery and nerve; the fascia iliaca is the hyperechoic layer over iliacus.
Use in-plane needling lateral to the nerve to keep the needle tip in view throughout.
A correct injection lifts the fascia off the muscle as an expanding hypoechoic lens.
If fluid pools within muscle or around the nerve without subfascial spread, reposition before injecting more.
confirmation: Local anesthetic seen spreading beneath the fascia iliaca on ultrasound
No intravascular or intraneural injection (negative aspiration, no spread within the nerve)
Total dose remained below the weight-based maximum
Progressive analgesia in the femoral distribution over the expected onset time
No signs of local anesthetic systemic toxicity
troubleshooting: Fluid spreading in muscle, not under the fascia: reposition the tip to the correct plane before continuing.
Resistance or the patient reports lancinating pain on injection: stop — possible intraneural position; withdraw and reassess.
Blood on aspiration: withdraw and reposition; never inject against a positive aspirate.
Early LAST signs (perioral numbness, tinnitus, agitation, dysrhythmia): stop injection and begin lipid-emulsion rescue.
Inadequate block despite good spread: confirm dose and onset time; supplement analgesia as needed within safe limits.
aftercare: Monitor for delayed LAST and reassess analgesia after the expected onset.
Document the block, dose, and post-block neurovascular exam.
Communicate the block to the orthopedic and inpatient teams so a femoral deficit is not misattributed.
Continue multimodal analgesia and reassess pain regularly.
documentation: Fascia iliaca compartment block performed for [hip/femur] fracture analgesia.
Contraindications excluded; pre-block distal neurovascular exam documented.
Local anesthetic agent, concentration, and total weight-based dose recorded (below maximum).
Ultrasound-guided subfascial spread confirmed; aspiration negative for blood.
No signs of LAST during or after the procedure.
Post-block analgesia and neurovascular status documented; block communicated to admitting team.
seniorPearls: This block shines in elderly hip fractures precisely because it spares the opioids that cause their delirium and respiratory trouble.
Subfascial spread is the whole game — fluid in muscle means no block and a wasted dose near your maximum.
Calculate the maximum dose every time and keep lipid emulsion in the room; large-volume blocks are where LAST happens.
references: ASRA guidance on regional anesthesia and management of local anesthetic systemic toxicity.
Emergency medicine regional anesthesia references on the fascia iliaca compartment block.
Institutional protocols on ED nerve blocks, dosing limits, and LAST rescue; verify local policy.

visualAssets.subtitle: Linear probe placed at the inguinal crease. Identify the femoral artery, fascia lata (superficial), and fascia iliaca (deep). Target the plane deep to fascia iliaca, lateral to the femoral nerve.

visualAssets.title: Inguinal fascial layers
visualAssets.subtitle: From superficial to deep: skin, subcutaneous fat, fascia lata, sartorius/iliopsoas, fascia iliaca, then the femoral nerve and iliacus muscle. The block targets the plane between fascia iliaca and iliacus.

visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Intravascular injection risk near femoral vessels. Aspirate before injecting.

shiftMode: This is a last-resort, time-critical trauma procedure with narrow indications — apply the criteria, do not improvise them.
Best evidence supports penetrating chest trauma with signs of life within a short window before arrest; blunt arrest outcomes are dismal.
Goals: release tamponade, control intrathoracic hemorrhage, cross-clamp the aorta, and allow internal cardiac massage.
Left anterolateral thoracotomy at the 4th/5th intercostal space; extend across the sternum (clamshell) for right-sided or wider access.
Open the pericardium longitudinally and anterior to the phrenic nerve to relieve tamponade.
Control obvious cardiac or great-vessel bleeding and cross-clamp the descending aorta if needed.
Have massive transfusion, surgical backup, and a clear destination (OR) activated before and during the procedure.
indications: Penetrating thoracic trauma with witnessed loss of vital signs and a short arrest interval
Traumatic arrest with suspected pericardial tamponade amenable to release
Selected penetrating trauma arrest with signs of life en route, per local trauma protocol
Exsanguinating intrathoracic hemorrhage where aortic cross-clamp may be life-saving as a bridge to the OR
contraindications: Blunt trauma arrest without signs of life (very poor outcomes; per protocol generally not indicated)
Prolonged pulselessness beyond the evidence-supported window
No realistic surgical/definitive care pathway available
Injuries clearly incompatible with survival
Penetrating trauma with prolonged arrest and no signs of life per local criteria
anatomy: Left anterolateral approach at the 4th-5th intercostal space follows the rib's superior margin to avoid the neurovascular bundle.
The pericardium is opened longitudinally and anterior to the phrenic nerve, which runs along its lateral surface.
The descending thoracic aorta lies posteriorly against the spine, anterior to it the esophagus and lung must be retracted.
Clamshell extension across the sternum exposes both pleural cavities and the heart for wider access.
equipment: Thoracotomy/clamshell tray (scalpel, Mayo scissors, rib spreader/Finochietto retractor)
Vascular clamps (aortic and DeBakey-type) for cross-clamping and vessel control
Suction and large volumes of gauze/laparotomy pads
Massive transfusion products and rapid infuser
Sterile gloves, gowns, and eye protection for the team
Foley catheter or sutures for temporary cardiac wound control
positioning: Patient supine with the left arm raised or abducted to expose the lateral chest.
Operate from the patient's left side for a left anterolateral thoracotomy.
Ensure continued airway management and ongoing resuscitation by the team.
Prep quickly; this is a time-critical procedure where speed and exposure dominate.
steps: Confirm the indication meets local resuscitative thoracotomy criteria and activate trauma surgery and massive transfusion.
Make a left anterolateral incision at the 4th-5th intercostal space along the superior rib margin, from sternum to the table.
Divide intercostal muscles and enter the pleura; place and open the rib spreader.
Extend across the sternum (clamshell) if right-sided injury or wider exposure is needed.
Open the pericardium longitudinally, anterior to the phrenic nerve, and evacuate clot to relieve tamponade.
Identify and control cardiac wounds with finger pressure, then a Foley balloon or sutures as appropriate.
Perform internal cardiac massage and internal defibrillation if indicated.
Cross-clamp the descending thoracic aorta to support coronary/cerebral perfusion and limit subdiaphragmatic hemorrhage.
Control intrathoracic bleeding and continue resuscitation with blood products.
Transport immediately to the operating room for definitive repair if circulation is restored.
ultrasound: Pre-arrest or peri-arrest ultrasound (e-FAST) can identify tamponade and guide the decision to proceed.
Cardiac standstill on ultrasound informs prognosis within the resuscitation algorithm.
Do not let imaging delay a clearly indicated thoracotomy in a peri-arrest penetrating chest trauma.
confirmation: Pericardium opened and tamponade relieved if present
Cardiac or great-vessel bleeding controlled
Aorta cross-clamped when indicated, with improved central perfusion
Return of organized cardiac activity or perfusing rhythm as the resuscitation endpoint
Patient moving to definitive operative care without delay
troubleshooting: Cannot find a cardiac wound after opening the pericardium: inspect the posterior surface and great vessels; lift the heart carefully.
Ongoing massive hemorrhage from the chest: pack, control the source, and cross-clamp the aorta while transfusing.
Phrenic nerve in the way of pericardiotomy: open anterior to it to avoid injury.
No response after pericardial release and aortic clamp: reassess for non-survivable injury and the resuscitation endpoint.
Right-sided injury inaccessible from the left: extend to a clamshell for bilateral exposure.
complications: Iatrogenic injury to the heart, lung, phrenic nerve, or great vessels
Coronary or aortic injury during clamping
Inadequate exposure leading to missed injury
Team blood-borne exposure during a rapid open procedure
Very high mortality given the underlying physiology
aftercare: Move immediately to the operating room for definitive repair if circulation returns.
Maintain the aortic cross-clamp time awareness and communicate it to the surgical team.
Continue massive transfusion and correct coagulopathy, acidosis, and hypothermia.
Debrief the team and address occupational exposures after the resuscitation.
documentation: Resuscitative thoracotomy performed for [penetrating/blunt] trauma arrest meeting local criteria.
Mechanism, arrest interval, and signs of life documented.
Procedure performed: thoracotomy ± clamshell, pericardiotomy, hemorrhage control, aortic cross-clamp as applicable.
Intraoperative findings and interventions recorded.
Resuscitation endpoint and disposition (OR vs. termination) documented.
Team exposures and debrief noted.
seniorPearls: The decision to open is the procedure — applying the indication criteria correctly matters more than blade speed.
Open the pericardium anterior to the phrenic nerve and longitudinally; tamponade release is often the single highest-yield action.
This is a bridge, not a destination: it buys minutes to reach the OR, and only certain patients can cross that bridge.
references: Western Trauma Association and EAST guidelines on emergency department/resuscitative thoracotomy indications.
ATLS and major trauma surgery references on resuscitative thoracotomy technique.
Institutional trauma protocols defining local indication criteria; verify local pathway.

visualAssets.subtitle: Anterolateral incision level, rib-margin entry, and longitudinal pericardial opening anterior to the phrenic nerve.
