visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Visual must make the phrenic-nerve-sparing pericardiotomy and incision level unambiguous.

shiftMode: IO is a bridge, not a destination. Transition to definitive IV access when stable.
Proximal tibia is the default site in adults. Proximal humerus is an alternative.
Flush with 10 mL saline after insertion to clear marrow and confirm flow.
Lidocaine 2% (preservative-free) through the IO before infusion reduces pain in conscious patients.
Nearly all IV medications and fluids can be given IO. Pressure bag or push-pull syringe for rapid infusion.
Remove within 24 hours. Document site, time in, and reason for IO.
indications: Failed or impossible peripheral IV access in emergent setting
Cardiac arrest
Critically ill or injured patient requiring immediate vascular access
Pediatric resuscitation when IV access not rapidly obtained
Severe dehydration with vascular collapse
contraindications: Fracture in the target bone
Prior IO attempt in the same bone within 24–48 hours
Overlying infection or burn at insertion site
Prosthetic joint or hardware at target site
Conditions with fragile bone (relative: osteogenesis imperfecta, severe osteoporosis)
anatomy: Proximal tibia: flat anteromedial surface, 1–2 cm distal and medial to the tibial tuberosity.
Proximal humerus: greater tubercle, with arm adducted and internally rotated (hand resting on abdomen).
Distal tibia: medial malleolus, 1–2 cm proximal to the most prominent point.
In pediatrics the proximal tibia is preferred; the flat medial tibial surface is more accessible.
equipment: IO drill device (EZ-IO or equivalent) with appropriate needle size
10 mL normal saline flush syringes (at least two)
positioning: Proximal tibia: leg slightly externally rotated, small towel roll behind the knee.
Proximal humerus: arm adducted, hand on abdomen to internally rotate.
Stabilize the limb. Do not place your hand behind the insertion point.
steps: Identify the insertion site and confirm no contraindications at that limb.
Clean the site with antiseptic.
Stabilize the limb without placing your hand behind the target bone.
Attach the appropriate needle to the drill driver.
Insert at 90 degrees to the bone surface. Drill until loss of resistance (a subtle give or pop).
Remove the trocar, attach a syringe, and aspirate (marrow aspirate confirms placement but is not always obtained).
Flush with 10 mL normal saline. Observe for swelling or extravasation.
In conscious patients, slowly inject 20–40 mg lidocaine 2% (preservative-free) IO and allow 60 seconds before flushing.
Connect IV tubing with extension set. Use pressure bag for rapid infusion.
Secure the needle and apply stabilizer dressing.
Document time of insertion, site, confirmation method, and complications.
confirmation: Needle stands upright without support after insertion
Loss of resistance felt during drilling
Marrow aspirate on syringe (not always present; absence does not rule out placement)
Saline flush flows freely without subcutaneous swelling
Medications and fluids infuse without extravasation
troubleshooting: No flow after insertion: ensure trocar removed, recheck position, attempt gentle flush. If extravasation seen, remove and attempt different bone.
Needle loose or wobbly: likely through-and-through cortex penetration. Remove and use a different site.
Significant pain in conscious patient: slow lidocaine injection through IO before infusion, allow dwell time.
Swelling at site: extravasation indicates malposition. Remove needle, compress, reassess compartment.
complications: Osteomyelitis (rare, risk increases if left in >24 hours)
Fracture (rare, more common with manual devices in pediatrics)
Pain during infusion in conscious patients
aftercare: Transition to peripheral or central IV access as soon as feasible.
Remove IO within 24 hours.
Document insertion time, site, removal time, and any complications.
Monitor insertion site for signs of infection, swelling, or compartment syndrome.
documentation: IO access placed at [site] using [device] for [indication].
Verbal/implied consent in emergent setting.
Site prepped, needle inserted with loss of resistance confirmed.
Flushed with [volume] normal saline without extravasation.
IO lidocaine given prior to infusion (if conscious).
Patient tolerated procedure. IO to be removed within 24 hours and transitioned to definitive access.
seniorPearls: IO is not a last resort. If you cannot get a peripheral line in a crashing patient in 90 seconds, drill.
The proximal humerus flows faster than the tibia in adults. Know both sites.
Lidocaine through the IO before infusion is a kindness that takes 60 seconds. Do it for conscious patients.
Through-and-through is a real risk in peds. Use the correct needle length and stop at loss of resistance.
references: Roberts and Hedges’ Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
ACLS/PALS guidelines on vascular access.
Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify device-specific instructions and institutional IO protocols.

visualAssets.title: Proximal tibia insertion site
visualAssets.subtitle: Palpate the tibial tuberosity. Move 1–2 cm distal and medial to the flat anteromedial tibial surface. This is the insertion point. Needle at 90 degrees to the bone.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Do not place your hand behind the limb during drilling.

visualAssets.title: Proximal humerus insertion site
visualAssets.subtitle: Arm adducted, hand on abdomen (internal rotation). Palpate the greater tubercle — the most prominent lateral bony point of the shoulder. Needle at 90 degrees to the bone surface.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Humeral IO flows faster than tibial in adults. Secure well — humeral IOs dislodge more easily.

shiftMode: Superolateral approach is the most common. Medial approach is an alternative.
Always send fluid for cell count, Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis.
Use sterile technique. A septic joint from your tap is a career-defining complication.
If you suspect septic arthritis, tap before antibiotics when feasible.
Ultrasound guidance increases success, especially in obese patients or small effusions.
Document appearance of fluid: clear, turbid, bloody, purulent.
indications: Acute monoarticular arthritis to evaluate for septic joint
Crystal arthropathy workup (gout, pseudogout)
Hemarthrosis evaluation
Symptomatic relief of tense effusion
Traumatic effusion with concern for intra-articular fracture or lipohemarthrosis
contraindications: Overlying cellulitis or skin infection at planned insertion site
Significant coagulopathy (relative; weigh risk vs. benefit if septic joint is suspected)
Prosthetic joint (consult orthopedics before tapping)
Patient refusal
anatomy: Superolateral approach: needle enters 1 cm superior and 1 cm lateral to the superolateral pole of the patella, directed toward the intercondylar notch.
Medial approach: needle enters at the midpoint of the patella on the medial side, directed posterolaterally.
The suprapatellar bursa communicates with the knee joint and is an alternative US-guided target.
Avoid the popliteal fossa (posterior neurovascular structures).
equipment: Local anesthetic (1% lidocaine), 5 mL syringe, 25G needle for skin wheal
18G needle and 20–60 mL syringe for aspiration
Specimen tubes: purple top (cell count), blood culture bottle or sterile tube (culture), green top or plain tube (crystals)
Ultrasound with high-frequency linear probe (if available)
positioning: Patient supine with knee in slight flexion (15–20 degrees). A rolled towel behind the knee helps.
Relaxed quadriceps. The patella should be mobile.
Identify landmarks or use ultrasound to confirm effusion location.
steps: Confirm indication and obtain consent.
Position the knee in slight flexion. Identify landmarks or use ultrasound.
Prep the insertion site with antiseptic using sterile technique.
Anesthetize the skin and subcutaneous tissue with lidocaine down to the capsule.
Insert the 18G needle at the chosen approach, advancing toward the joint space while applying gentle negative pressure on the syringe.
Aspirate fluid. If using a large syringe, a hemostat can stabilize the needle during syringe exchange.
Remove as much fluid as clinically appropriate for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.
Remove the needle and apply sterile dressing.
Send fluid for cell count with differential, Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis.
Document volume, appearance, and studies sent.
ultrasound: High-frequency linear probe placed over the suprapatellar recess (longitudinal) to confirm effusion.
Anechoic fluid collection confirms effusion. Complex or echogenic fluid may suggest infection or hemorrhage.
Use in-plane needle guidance for real-time visualization.
Particularly useful in obese patients or when clinical exam is equivocal.
confirmation: Synovial fluid aspirated into syringe
Knee effusion clinically reduced after aspiration
Fluid sent for appropriate laboratory studies
troubleshooting: Dry tap: reposition needle, use ultrasound guidance, consider smaller effusion or loculated collection.
Bloody aspirate: may be hemarthrosis or traumatic tap. Send for analysis. Lipohemarthrosis on gross inspection suggests intra-articular fracture.
Difficulty advancing needle: may be hitting bone. Redirect slightly.
Patient unable to relax quadriceps: coach relaxation, ensure adequate local anesthesia.
complications: Iatrogenic septic arthritis (rare with proper sterile technique)
aftercare: Instruct patient on signs of infection: increasing pain, redness, warmth, fever.
Keep dressing clean and dry for 24 hours.
Follow up on culture and crystal results.
If septic arthritis suspected, coordinate disposition and antibiotics appropriately.
documentation: Knee arthrocentesis performed on [side] knee for [indication].
Informed consent obtained.
Site prepped with sterile technique. Local anesthesia administered.
Aspirated [volume] mL of [appearance] fluid using [approach].
Fluid sent for cell count, Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis.
Patient tolerated procedure without immediate complication.
Sterile dressing applied.
seniorPearls: If you are debating whether to tap a hot swollen joint, tap it. Missing a septic joint is worse than a dry tap.
Ultrasound turns a frustrating blind procedure into a reliable one. Use it.
Clear straw-colored fluid at the bedside does not rule out infection. Send the labs.
A white count over 50,000 with >90% PMNs is septic until proven otherwise, regardless of crystals.
references: Roberts and Hedges’ Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify institutional policy on anticoagulation and prosthetic joint aspiration.

visualAssets.title: Superolateral approach
visualAssets.subtitle: 1 cm superior and 1 cm lateral to the superolateral pole of the patella. Direct the needle toward the intercondylar notch, posterior and slightly inferior.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Avoid posterior approach (popliteal neurovascular bundle).

visualAssets.title: Suprapatellar recess ultrasound
visualAssets.subtitle: High-frequency linear probe placed longitudinally over the suprapatellar recess. Anechoic fluid between the quadriceps tendon and femur confirms effusion.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: Complex or echogenic fluid may indicate infection or hemarthrosis.

shiftMode: Most anterior epistaxis is from Kiesselbach plexus (Little area) on the septum.
Start with direct pressure: pinch the soft part of the nose for 15 minutes continuously.
If a single bleeding point is visible, silver nitrate cautery may stop the bleed without packing.
If packing is needed, Rapid Rhino or Merocel sponge are first-line devices.
Always check vitals and hemodynamic stability. Posterior bleeds and anticoagulated patients can hemorrhage.
Patients discharged with packing need ENT follow-up for removal in 48–72 hours.
indications: Anterior epistaxis uncontrolled by direct pressure
Visible anterior bleeding source amenable to packing
Recurrent epistaxis requiring temporizing control
Post-cautery reinforcement when bleeding recurs
contraindications: Suspected posterior epistaxis (pack both if unavoidable, but involve ENT early)
Suspected nasal fracture with CSF leak (relative; manage with specialist)
Severe midface trauma requiring operative management
anatomy: Kiesselbach plexus (Little area): anterior septum, the most common source of anterior epistaxis.
Supplied by branches of anterior ethmoidal, sphenopalatine, superior labial, and greater palatine arteries.
The nasal cavity narrows posteriorly. Packing must fill the anterior space to tamponade the vessel.
Direct packing along the nasal floor, not upward toward the cribriform plate.
equipment: Personal protective equipment (gown, gloves, face shield)
Topical vasoconstrictor (oxymetazoline spray or phenylephrine-soaked pledgets)
Topical anesthetic (4% lidocaine or lidocaine/epinephrine mix on cotton pledgets)
Silver nitrate sticks (for cautery if appropriate)
Nasal packing device: Rapid Rhino, Merocel sponge, or ribbon gauze with petroleum jelly
Syringe with water or saline for inflating balloon devices
Kidney basin and gauze for blood management
positioning: Patient seated upright leaning slightly forward to prevent blood swallowing.
Have the patient blow out clots gently before examination.
Apply topical vasoconstrictor and anesthetic pledgets for 10–15 minutes before instrumentation.
steps: Ensure hemodynamic stability. Obtain IV access if significant bleeding or anticoagulation.
Apply personal protective equipment.
Have the patient blow out clots. Suction the nasal cavity.
Apply topical vasoconstrictor and anesthetic pledgets. Wait 10–15 minutes.
Remove pledgets and inspect with nasal speculum and headlamp to identify bleeding source.
If a single anterior point is identified and bleeding is controlled: apply silver nitrate to the site for 5–10 seconds. Do not cauterize both sides of the septum simultaneously (perforation risk).
If cautery fails or bleeding is diffuse: proceed to nasal packing.
Rapid Rhino: coat with water to activate lubricant, insert along nasal floor, inflate balloon with air or saline per device instructions.
Merocel sponge: insert dry sponge along the nasal floor, then hydrate with saline to expand.
Ribbon gauze: layer gauze accordion-style from floor to ceiling of nasal cavity using bayonet forceps, coat with antibiotic ointment or petroleum jelly.
Confirm hemostasis. Check for posterior drainage in the oropharynx.
If bilateral packing is placed, monitor for hypoxia in high-risk patients.
confirmation: Active bleeding has stopped
No posterior drainage seen in the oropharynx on inspection
Packing is seated properly and not extruding
Patient hemodynamically stable
troubleshooting: Continued bleeding after packing: ensure packing fills the anterior cavity adequately. May need additional packing or posterior epistaxis management.
Posterior bleed suspected: blood draining in posterior pharynx despite adequate anterior packing. Place posterior balloon or consult ENT urgently.
Patient on anticoagulation: correct coagulopathy as able while packing. Check INR/platelet count.
Vasovagal during packing: recline patient, maintain airway, resume when stable.
complications: Nasal septal necrosis or pressure injury (if packing too tight or left too long)
Sinusitis or toxic shock syndrome (rare, from prolonged packing)
aftercare: Discharge with packing in place. Arrange ENT follow-up for removal in 48–72 hours.
Prescribe antibiotics covering for toxic shock syndrome prevention per institutional protocol (controversial; follow local practice).
Instruct patient to keep head elevated, avoid straining, nose blowing, and blood thinners if safe to hold.
Return precautions: recurrent heavy bleeding, difficulty breathing, fever, significant swelling.
documentation: Anterior epistaxis managed with [method: cautery / packing device].
Bleeding source identified on [side] anterior septum.
Topical vasoconstrictor and anesthetic applied.
[Cautery performed / Packing placed] with hemostasis achieved.
No posterior drainage observed.
Patient hemodynamically stable. Discharged with ENT follow-up arranged.
Return precautions given.
seniorPearls: Fifteen minutes of real continuous pressure stops most anterior bleeds. Patients never actually do this before they arrive.
If you cannot see the bleeding source, you cannot fix it. Suction, vasoconstrict, and light the field before touching anything.
Do not cauterize both sides of the septum. One side today, the other side never.
A posterior bleed masquerading as anterior packing failure will waste your time until you check the oropharynx.
references: Roberts and Hedges’ Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify institutional policy on antibiotic prophylaxis with nasal packing and cautery technique.

shiftMode: Synchronized cardioversion delivers a shock timed to the R wave. Always confirm SYNC mode is on before shocking.
Unstable = cardiovert now. Stable = consider rate control, rhythm control, or elective cardioversion.
Signs of instability: hypotension, altered mental status, ischemic chest pain, acute heart failure.
Sedate the patient before cardioversion unless peri-arrest.
If the patient deteriorates into pulseless VT or VF, switch to defibrillation (SYNC off, max energy).
Starting energy: atrial fibrillation 120–200 J biphasic; atrial flutter/SVT 50–100 J; VT with pulse 100 J.
indications: Unstable tachyarrhythmia with a pulse (SVT, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, monomorphic VT with a pulse)
Elective cardioversion of persistent atrial fibrillation or flutter after appropriate anticoagulation
Wide complex tachycardia with hemodynamic compromise
contraindications: Pulseless VT or VF (requires unsynchronized defibrillation, not cardioversion)
Digitalis toxicity–related arrhythmia (relative; cardioversion may precipitate refractory VF)
Multifocal atrial tachycardia (will not respond; treat underlying cause)
Sinus tachycardia (not an indication for cardioversion; treat the cause)
anatomy: Anterolateral pad placement: right parasternal and left lateral chest wall (V5–V6 position at the mid-axillary line).
Anteroposterior placement: sternum and left infrascapular area. May be superior for atrial fibrillation.
Ensure pads do not overlap implanted devices (pacemaker, ICD). Place pads at least 8 cm from the generator.
equipment: Continuous cardiac monitor with 12-lead ECG capability
Sedation medications (etomidate, propofol, midazolam, or ketamine per institutional protocol)
Airway equipment including bag-valve mask, oral airway, suction, and intubation supplies at bedside
Resuscitation medications (epinephrine, amiodarone, atropine) at bedside
positioning: Patient supine. Pads placed with good skin contact (shave excess chest hair if needed, wipe moisture).
Clear the area around the patient before shock delivery.
Ensure monitor leads are connected and rhythm is visible.
steps: Confirm the indication: identify the rhythm, confirm hemodynamic instability or elective plan.
Obtain 12-lead ECG if time permits.
Establish IV access. Place monitoring (pulse ox, capnography, cardiac monitor).
Prepare airway equipment at bedside.
Apply defibrillation pads in anterolateral or anteroposterior position.
Administer procedural sedation (if patient is conscious and not peri-arrest). Confirm adequate sedation depth.
Select energy level based on rhythm. Activate SYNC mode on the defibrillator.
Verify SYNC markers are tracking R waves on the monitor.
Announce "Charging" and charge the defibrillator.
Perform a visual and verbal safety check: "I am clear, you are clear, everyone is clear."
Deliver the shock. Hold the shock button until the device fires (SYNC mode may have a brief delay).
Assess the rhythm immediately. If unsuccessful, increase energy and repeat. Re-select SYNC mode (many devices default back to defibrillation after each shock).
If the patient becomes pulseless, immediately switch to defibrillation protocol.
confirmation: Post-shock rhythm is converted to sinus or target rhythm
Hemodynamic improvement (blood pressure, mentation, perfusion)
Repeat 12-lead ECG shows new rhythm
troubleshooting: Shock does not fire: SYNC mode waits for an R wave. If the rhythm is too irregular or amplitude is too low, the device may not fire. Try a different lead, increase ECG gain, or switch to unsynchronized defibrillation if the patient is deteriorating.
Rhythm unchanged after shock: increase energy, ensure good pad contact, try anteroposterior pad position.
Patient deteriorates to VF or pulseless VT: immediately switch to unsynchronized defibrillation at max energy.
Post-cardioversion bradycardia: observe, atropine if symptomatic, transcutaneous pacing at bedside.
Recurrence of arrhythmia: consider antiarrhythmic medication before repeating cardioversion.
complications: Thromboembolism (stroke) if atrial fibrillation cardioverted without adequate anticoagulation
aftercare: Monitor on telemetry for at least 1–3 hours post-cardioversion.
Repeat 12-lead ECG.
Assess for chest wall discomfort, skin burns at pad sites.
Resume or initiate anticoagulation and antiarrhythmic therapy as indicated.
If elective cardioversion for atrial fibrillation: ensure appropriate anticoagulation plan before discharge.
documentation: Synchronized cardioversion performed for [rhythm] with [indication].
Pre-procedure rhythm documented on 12-lead ECG.
Sedation: [agent, dose, route]. Adequate sedation achieved.
SYNC mode confirmed. [Energy] J delivered via biphasic defibrillator.
Post-shock rhythm: [rhythm]. Hemodynamics [stable/improved].
Patient tolerated procedure without immediate complication.
Post-procedure 12-lead ECG obtained. Monitoring plan documented.
seniorPearls: SYNC mode resets after every shock on most devices. Re-select it every single time or you will defibrillate a patient with a pulse.
The shock button delay in SYNC mode confuses people. Hold the button until it fires.
Anteroposterior pad placement is better for atrial fibrillation. If anterolateral fails, switch before escalating energy.
Do not cardiovert sinus tachycardia. Find and fix the cause.
If the rhythm is polymorphic VT, treat it as VF: unsynchronized defibrillation, not synchronized cardioversion.
references: ACLS guidelines on tachycardia management.
AHA/ACC guidelines on atrial fibrillation management.
Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify institutional cardioversion and sedation protocols.

shiftMode: Needle aspiration is the first-line ED drainage technique. Most PTAs can be aspirated at bedside.
The danger: the internal carotid artery lies posterolateral to the tonsil, typically 2–2.5 cm from the mucosal surface.
Limit needle depth to 1 cm with a guard (cut syringe sheath or tape) to prevent deep penetration.
Aspirate at the point of maximal fluctuance or bulging, usually the superior pole of the tonsil.
Always have suction ready. These patients aspirate pus.
If you cannot aspirate pus but clinical suspicion is high, consider CT with contrast or ENT consultation.
indications: Clinically suspected peritonsillar abscess (unilateral tonsillar swelling, uvular deviation, trismus, muffled voice)
Confirmed peritonsillar abscess on imaging or clinical exam
Failed outpatient antibiotic therapy for peritonsillar cellulitis progressing to abscess
contraindications: Concern for deep space neck infection extending beyond the peritonsillar space (consult ENT/surgery)
Pulsatile mass or bruit suggesting vascular anomaly (get imaging first)
Severe trismus preventing adequate visualization (may need sedation or operative drainage)
Patient unable to cooperate (relative; consider procedural sedation)
anatomy: The peritonsillar space lies between the tonsillar capsule and the superior pharyngeal constrictor muscle.
The internal carotid artery runs posterolateral, typically 2–2.5 cm from the pharyngeal mucosal surface.
Most PTAs collect at the superior pole of the tonsil, between the anterior tonsillar pillar and the soft palate.
Uvular deviation is typically away from the affected side.
The palatine tonsil, anterior and posterior pillars, and soft palate are key landmarks.
equipment: Local anesthetic: lidocaine 1% with epinephrine, 3 mL syringe, 25G needle
Aspiration: 18G needle on a 10 mL syringe
Needle guard: tape or cut syringe cap placed 1 cm from needle tip
positioning: Patient seated upright, leaning slightly forward.
Good light source directed at the oropharynx.
Tongue depressor in the non-dominant hand.
Suction immediately available.
Have the patient open mouth maximally. If trismus limits opening, topical anesthetic and gentle approach or sedation may be needed.
steps: Confirm the diagnosis clinically: unilateral tonsillar swelling, uvular deviation, muffled voice, trismus.
Apply topical anesthetic spray to the area of planned aspiration.
Inject local anesthetic (1% lidocaine with epinephrine) into the mucosa overlying the abscess. Aspirate first to exclude vascular puncture.
Attach an 18G needle to a 10 mL syringe. Place a needle guard at 1 cm from the tip.
Insert the needle at the point of maximal fluctuance (usually the superior pole, at the junction of the anterior pillar and soft palate).
Aspirate while advancing slowly up to 1 cm only.
If pus is obtained: aspirate as much as possible. Send sample for culture.
If no pus is obtained: redirect the needle slightly inferior or medial. A second attempt at the middle pole may be tried.
If no pus after 2–3 attempts: consider CT to confirm abscess vs. cellulitis, or consult ENT.
After drainage, have the patient gargle and spit. Reassess the oropharynx.
ultrasound: Intraoral ultrasound (endocavitary probe with a cover) can identify the abscess collection and confirm depth.
Transcutaneous submandibular approach may help identify large collections.
Particularly useful when clinical exam is equivocal or prior aspiration was dry.
confirmation: Purulent material aspirated
Improvement in uvular deviation and tonsillar swelling
Patient reports subjective improvement in swallowing and pain
Reduced trismus
troubleshooting: Dry tap: reposition needle to a different quadrant. Consider imaging if repeated attempts fail.
Trismus limiting access: topical anesthetic, consider procedural sedation, or refer to ENT for operative drainage.
Significant bleeding: apply pressure with gauze, maintain suction, most minor mucosal bleeding is self-limited.
Large abscess with rapid re-accumulation: ENT consultation for incision and drainage or tonsillectomy.
complications: Aspiration of pus into the airway
Injury to the internal carotid artery (prevented by needle depth guard)
Infection spread to deep neck spaces
aftercare: Oral antibiotics: amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin. Follow institutional guidelines.
Pain management: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, consider medrol dose pack per institutional protocol.
Encourage oral hydration. Soft diet.
ENT follow-up within 2–3 days for reassessment.
Return precautions: worsening pain, inability to swallow, difficulty breathing, fever, neck swelling.
documentation: Peritonsillar abscess drainage via needle aspiration performed.
Informed consent obtained.
Topical and local anesthesia applied to [side] peritonsillar area.
Needle inserted at superior pole with depth guard at 1 cm.
Aspirated [volume] mL of purulent material. Sent for culture.
Patient tolerated procedure without complication.
Antibiotics prescribed. ENT follow-up arranged. Return precautions discussed.
seniorPearls: The needle guard is not optional. The carotid is 2 cm away. You only need 1 cm of depth.
If the patient cannot open their mouth, do not force it. Sedate or consult ENT.
Most PTAs are at the superior pole. If your first aspiration is dry, try there before giving up.
These patients look terrible but get dramatically better within minutes of drainage. That immediate relief is diagnostic and therapeutic.
references: Roberts and Hedges’ Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Verify institutional antibiotic guidelines and ENT referral pathways.

shiftMode: X-ray detects glass, metal, gravel, and bone. It does not reliably detect wood, plastic, or thorns.
Ultrasound is the best bedside tool for radiolucent foreign bodies (wood, plastic, thorns).
Adequate anesthesia and a bloodless field make the difference between a controlled extraction and a dig.
If you cannot find it in a reasonable time or it is near critical structures, stop and refer.
Document what you found, what you removed, and what imaging showed. If retained FB is possible, tell the patient.
Tetanus status and wound infection risk should be addressed at every visit.
indications: Known or suspected retained foreign body in soft tissue
Wound with mechanism suggesting foreign body (glass, wood, metal)
Non-healing wound or recurrent infection suggesting retained material
Patient-reported foreign body sensation
contraindications: Foreign body near major neurovascular structures requiring operative exploration
Deep foreign body not accessible with bedside techniques
Foreign body that is asymptomatic, deeply embedded, and not causing infection (may be observed in select cases)
Inadequate anesthesia or equipment for safe removal
anatomy: Knowledge of local neurovascular anatomy is essential for safe exploration.
Hand and foot foreign bodies are near tendons, nerves, and vessels in tight compartments.
Plantar foot: flexor tendons, digital nerves, and plantar fascia are at risk during deep exploration.
The depth and tissue plane of the foreign body dictate the approach. Imaging helps plan.
equipment: Local anesthetic (1% or 2% lidocaine with or without epinephrine)
Wound irrigation supplies (saline, 18G angiocath on 30–60 mL syringe)
Tourniquet or Penrose drain (for digit bloodless field)
Suture material for closure if needed
positioning: Position the affected area for optimal access, lighting, and comfort.
Use a tourniquet for digit procedures to create a bloodless field.
Ensure the patient is comfortable and the field is well-lit.
steps: Obtain history: mechanism, timing, material type, symptoms since injury.
Examine the wound. Assess for signs of infection, neurovascular status distally.
Image the area: X-ray for radiopaque materials, ultrasound for radiolucent objects.
Mark the foreign body location on the skin using imaging guidance if needed.
Anesthetize the area with local or regional block as appropriate.
Prep and drape the wound.
If the foreign body is visible or superficial: extract with forceps.
If deeper: make a small incision over the marked location. Blunt dissect along tissue planes using hemostats.
Use ultrasound in real-time to guide your approach to deeper or radiolucent foreign bodies.
Grasp and remove the foreign body. Inspect it for fragmentation.
Irrigate the wound copiously with normal saline.
Reassess the wound. Obtain post-removal imaging if fragmentation is suspected.
Close the wound if appropriate (clean, non-infected, within time window). Leave contaminated or infected wounds open.
Apply appropriate dressing.
ultrasound: High-frequency linear probe (10–15 MHz) provides best resolution for superficial foreign bodies.
Foreign bodies appear as hyperechoic structures, often with posterior shadowing (glass, metal) or reverberation artifact.
Wood may appear as a hyperechoic linear structure with dirty shadowing.
Scan in two orthogonal planes to localize in 3D.
Real-time ultrasound guidance during extraction improves success for deep or small foreign bodies.
confirmation: Foreign body visualized and removed intact
Post-removal imaging shows no retained fragments (if indicated)
Wound irrigated and inspected
Neurovascular status intact after removal
troubleshooting: Cannot locate foreign body: reimage with ultrasound, try different scanning plane, consider CT for deep or small objects.
Fragmented foreign body: remove what is accessible, image to assess residual, discuss with patient and consider specialist referral.
Significant bleeding during exploration: apply direct pressure, tourniquet for digits, consider specialist referral for deep structures.
Foreign body near nerve or tendon: stop if safe extraction is not possible. Refer to hand surgery or appropriate specialist.
complications: Excessive tissue damage from prolonged exploration
aftercare: Update tetanus immunization if indicated.
Antibiotics for contaminated wounds, bite wounds, or immunocompromised patients per guidelines.
Wound care instructions: keep clean, dressing changes, elevation.
Return precautions: increasing pain, redness, swelling, drainage, fever.
If retained FB is possible: disclose to patient, arrange follow-up, document discussion.
documentation: Foreign body removal performed from [location].
Informed consent obtained.
Pre-procedure imaging: [modality] showing [finding].
Local anesthesia administered. Sterile technique used.
Foreign body identified and removed: [description of object].
Wound irrigated with [volume] normal saline.
Post-procedure neurovascular status intact.
[Wound closed with X / wound left open for healing by secondary intention].
Tetanus status addressed. Return precautions and follow-up discussed.
seniorPearls: If you are digging for more than 15 minutes, you are not removing a foreign body. You are creating a bigger wound.
Ultrasound finds what X-ray misses. Wood and plastic are invisible on plain film but light up on ultrasound.
The tourniquet on a finger transforms a bloody mess into a clean field. Use it.
Telling a patient there might be a retained fragment is better than a malpractice deposition about one you missed.
references: Roberts and Hedges’ Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care.
Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide.
Emergency Ultrasound Guidelines from ACEP.
Verify institutional wound management and antibiotic prophylaxis guidelines.

visualAssets.title: Foreign body on ultrasound
visualAssets.subtitle: High-frequency linear probe. Foreign bodies appear as hyperechoic structures with posterior shadowing (glass, metal) or reverberation artifact. Wood shows dirty shadowing. Scan in two planes.
visualAssets.caption: Replace assetName with reviewed artwork when available.
visualAssets.clinicalWarning: X-ray misses wood, plastic, and thorns. Ultrasound is the bedside tool for radiolucent foreign bodies.
